topics.title: Design Concepts
topics.description: Principles and elements of visual design used to create effective digital products.

blocks.text: Every visual design is built from seven fundamental building blocks — the elements of design. Before you touch Figma or Canva, you need to recognise these in the wild and understand what each one communicates.

blocks.heading: Line — guides the eye
blocks.html: Line is the most basic design element but one of the most expressive. The direction, weight, and style of a line communicate mood before the viewer reads a single word.

blocks.heading: Shape — carries personality
blocks.html: Banks use squares and rectangles (stability). Tech startups use circles (approachability). Organic brands use irregular blobs (natural). Shape is a brand decision.

blocks.heading: Space — the power of empty
blocks.html: White space is not wasted space — it's a premium signal. Apple built a $3 trillion brand on generous negative space. Cramped = cheap. Spacious = luxurious.

blocks.heading: Colour — the fastest signal
blocks.html: Every colour has three independent properties. Changing one changes meaning. High saturation = vivid energy. Low saturation = muted sophistication. Low tone contrast = accessibility failure.

blocks.heading: Tone — the greyscale test
blocks.html: Tone is lightness vs darkness, independent of colour. Run every design through a greyscale filter before launch. If your button disappears in greyscale, it fails for users with colour blindness.

question.text: A health brand uses light grey text on a white background. Which element of design is failing, and why?
options: Colour — the hue is wrong for healthcare
Shape — the layout is too geometric
Tone — insufficient contrast between light grey and white
Space — there is not enough padding
question.explanation: Tone — the lightness/darkness contrast between light grey and white is too low. This fails WCAG accessibility standards (minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text). The hue (grey) may be fine, but the tone difference is too small to read reliably.

question.text: White space in a design is wasted space that should always be filled with content.
question.explanation: False. White space (negative space) is a deliberate design decision. It reduces visual clutter, improves readability, and signals premium quality. Apple, Google, and every major tech brand uses generous white space intentionally.

blocks.text: If elements are the ingredients, principles are the recipe. Knowing what's in the dish doesn't mean you can cook it. Principles tell you HOW to combine elements so the result works for the user.

blocks.heading: Balance — distribute visual weight
blocks.html: Symmetrical balance = formal, corporate, safe. Asymmetrical balance = dynamic, modern, editorial. Neither is better — know which one matches the client's brand.

blocks.heading: Emphasis + Dominance — one hero per screen
blocks.html: If everything is important, nothing is. Every screen needs ONE dominant element that catches the eye first. Your CTA is the hero. Make it obvious — then make everything else step back.

blocks.heading: Contrast — make differences clear
blocks.html: Contrast isn't just visual preference — it's an accessibility requirement. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Failing this means your site is legally inaccessible to users with vision impairment.

blocks.heading: Unity — one visual language
blocks.html: Unity is what a brand design system enforces. One font family. One colour palette. Consistent corner radius on all buttons and cards. When every element belongs to the same visual family, the design feels intentional.

blocks.heading: Proportion — size communicates importance
blocks.html: Size relationships communicate hierarchy instantly. H1 > H2 > body > caption. A heading the same size as body text has no proportion — so it has no hierarchy. Eyeballing it is for students. Use a type scale.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Leo's Diagnostic Framework
blocks.text: Before any critique, run through every principle in order: Is there ONE dominant focal point? Is the layout balanced? Does contrast meet WCAG? Is the visual language unified? Are proportions communicating hierarchy? Missing any one of these is why designs feel 'off' even when people can't say why.

question.text: A student's landing page has a headline, hero image, 4 columns of body text, 3 CTA buttons, and 2 banner images — all the same visual weight. Which principle is most critically missing?
options: Unity — the visual language is inconsistent
Emphasis — there is no single dominant focal point
Texture — the page lacks visual surface variety
Balance — the columns are uneven
question.explanation: Emphasis — when every element has the same visual weight, there's no focal point, no hierarchy, and no clear action for the user. The eye doesn't know where to go. Leo's rule: one hero, one message, one primary CTA per screen.

question.text: Look at two website screenshots: Site A uses black text on white (#000 on #FFF). Site B uses light grey text on white (#D1D5DB on #FFFFFF). Both use the same font size. Explain which site has a contrast problem, identify which design principle is violated, and state the accessibility standard it fails.
question.explanation: Site B has the contrast problem. Principle violated: Contrast (and tone). #D1D5DB on #FFFFFF has a contrast ratio of approximately 1.6:1 — far below the WCAG AA minimum of 4.5:1 for normal body text. This makes the content inaccessible to users with low vision or colour blindness. Site A's #000 on #FFF achieves 21:1 — perfect contrast.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #0058 · KOOMBANA CREATIVE LABS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 HIGH | Sprint 4 | Assigned to: New Intern

A local Bunbury brewery wants a rebrand. The founder sent Leo a mood board with 14 different fonts. 'I want it to feel handcrafted but also professional.' Leo needs you to cut this to 2 fonts, define the typographic system, and brief the founder in plain language why the other 12 were cut.

blocks.title: Beginning Graphic Design: Typography
blocks.caption: GCFLearnFree — Leo's go-to primer before any typography brief.

blocks.text: Typography is the design element that most non-designers abuse and most senior designers obsess over. Bad font choices communicate distrust, chaos, or inaccessibility before the user reads a single word.

items.label: Serif
items.description: Small strokes at letter ends (Georgia, Times). Traditional, trustworthy, editorial. Law firms, newspapers, finance. Excellent for print body text.

items.label: Sans-serif
items.description: No strokes — clean and modern (Roboto, Inter, Helvetica). Dominant in tech and UI. Every major OS default UI font is sans-serif. Your default choice for screens.

items.label: Script / Handwritten
items.description: Mimics handwriting (Pacifico, Dancing Script). Warm, personal, artisanal. The brewery mood board is full of these. Used ONLY for headings, max one per design.

items.label: Display / Decorative
items.description: Eye-catching, highly stylised (Impact, Bebas Neue). Loud and specific. Perfect for event posters. Never for body text. Never more than one per project.

rows: Body text = left. Centred = signage/headings only. Justified = almost never for digital.
1.4–1.6× font size for readable body text. Tight = posters. Loose = long-form reading.
Tracking expanded on ALL CAPS for logo treatment. Kerning fixed for specific awkward pairs.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · THE BREWERY BRIEF
blocks.text:
The founder's 14 fonts have been narrowed to 4 finalists:
1. Playfair Display (serif) — 'elegant, premium'
2. Pacifico (script) — 'handcrafted, warm'
3. Inter (sans-serif) — 'clean, digital'
4. Bebas Neue (display) — 'bold, industrial'

Your job: Pick exactly 2. Write your type system:
- Heading font: ___  (Why? One sentence)
- Body font: ___  (Why? One sentence)
- Fonts you cut and why: ___

Bonus: Leo will ask what font pairing rule makes your system work. Have the answer ready.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · LEO IS CHECKING YOUR PAIRING
blocks.text:
Leo reviewed your selection. Comments in the thread:

Comment 1: 'You chose a script font for the heading. Tell me one rule you must follow about using script fonts for accessibility.'
Comment 2: 'Your body font is 16px. A developer on your team wants to compress it to 12px to fit more content. Give them three reasons why this is wrong.'
Comment 3: 'The founder wants the menu items in ALL CAPS. You've added spacing between the letters. What typography property did you adjust, and why does it help?'

question.text: A graduate designer uses a script font at 10px for a website's body text. What are the TWO most critical problems?
options: Brand inconsistency and wrong colour
Readability at small sizes and inaccessibility for users with dyslexia
It's too expensive to license and slow to load
Wrong alignment and no contrast
question.explanation: Script fonts have inconsistent stroke widths that become illegible below ~24px. They're also one of the hardest typefaces for dyslexic users to read. These are functional failures, not just aesthetic preferences — they make your interface inaccessible.

question.text: Using more than two typeface families in a single design system makes it feel more professional and varied.
question.explanation: False — the professional rule is two typeface families maximum. Additional fonts create visual noise and brand inconsistency. Inter + Playfair Display. That's it. Variety within a system comes from weight, size, and colour — not from adding more fonts.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · LEO THORNE, KOOMBANA CREATIVE LABS
blocks.text: In ten years I've never once heard a user say 'I love this site because it has seven different fonts.' I have heard 'this feels chaotic and I don't trust it.' Typography is 95% of design because text is 95% of the internet. Two fonts, clear hierarchy, generous leading, left-aligned body text. That's not a limitation — that's professional discipline. The craft is in the details, not the variety.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #0071 · KOOMBANA CREATIVE LABS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟢 STANDARD | Sprint 5 | Assigned to: New Intern

A Margaret River photography studio wants you to select 3 hero images from a set of 10 they've provided for their new homepage. Leo needs you to apply compositional evaluation criteria, score each image, and give the studio a written rationale for the three you selected.

blocks.title: Three Compositional Rules Every Designer Uses

items.label: Rule of Thirds
items.description: Divide canvas into 3×3. Place focal point at an intersection — not dead centre. Off-centre subjects feel natural, dynamic, and intentional. The most-used rule in photography and web design.

items.label: Grid & Alignment
items.description: All elements align to invisible grid lines. In CSS this is Grid and Flexbox. In Figma it's auto-layout. Consistent alignment = professional. Random placement = student project.

items.label: Golden Ratio
items.description: 1:1.618 — found in nature, architecture, and logo design. Governs ideal proportions for layout sections, font-size scales (e.g. 16/26/42px follows the ratio), and image cropping.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · IMAGE SCORING RUBRIC
blocks.text:
Create a scoring rubric for the photography studio. Score each compositional rule out of 5. Then define:

1. What does a 5/5 'Rule of Thirds' image look like? (One sentence)
2. What does a 1/5 'Rule of Thirds' image look like? (One sentence)
3. An image scores 5/5 on composition but the subject is slightly blurry. Does it still qualify as a hero image? Why or why not?
4. The studio insists on using an image where the subject is dead centre. Make the design case for why this is wrong — then describe ONE situation where a centred composition is actually intentional and correct.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · LEO IS CHECKING YOUR RATIONALE
blocks.text:
Leo reviewed your image selections. His comments:

Comment 1: 'You selected Image 7 which has the horizon dead centre. You said it was balanced. But which compositional rule does this violate, and how does it affect the user's emotional response?'
Comment 2: 'Image 3 follows rule of thirds perfectly but the grid alignment on the website would put text over the subject's face. How do you solve this while preserving the compositional rule?'

question.text: A photographer places the horizon line exactly in the middle of every landscape photo. Which compositional rule are they ignoring?
options: Golden ratio
Grid and alignment
Rule of thirds
Colour harmony
question.explanation: Rule of thirds — placing the horizon at the top or bottom third creates a more dynamic, intentional image. Dead-centre horizon creates tension and feels static. The rule of thirds is the first compositional rule taught to every photography and design student for this exact reason.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · LEO THORNE, KOOMBANA CREATIVE LABS
blocks.text: Rules are guidelines. Break them deliberately, not by accident. I've seen centred compositions become iconic logos. I've seen 'incorrect' grids create masterpiece album covers. The difference is intent and mastery. Learn the rules until they're instinct. Then — and only then — break them on purpose, and be ready to defend every decision in the client meeting.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Analysing and Applying Design
Being able to analyse existing designs and then apply those principles to your own work is a core skill in AIT. This lesson brings together all the design concepts from this topic.
How to Analyse a Design
Use a structured approach when analysing any digital product. Work through elements, then principles, then overall effectiveness.

items: Identify the purpose and audience of the design
List the design elements present (line, shape, colour, space, typography...)
Identify which design principles have been applied (contrast, alignment, proximity...)
Evaluate how effectively the design communicates its message
Suggest at least one improvement and explain why it would help

blocks.text: Design for Purpose and Audience

Every design decision should serve the purpose of the product and the needs of the target audience. A design for primary school children needs large fonts, bright colours, and simple layouts. A design for legal professionals needs formal typography, neutral colours, and dense information.
SCSA exams frequently ask you to "justify" design decisions. Always connect your choice to purpose and audience — not just personal preference.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Professional IT workers keep a log of every issue they encounter and how they solved it. This becomes an invaluable personal knowledge base.
Designing for Accessibility

Accessible design ensures digital products can be used by people with disabilities. Key accessibility considerations include: sufficient colour contrast, not relying on colour alone to convey meaning, readable font sizes, alt text for images, and logical heading structure.

blocks.definition: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — international standards for making web content accessible to people with disabilities.

blocks.text: The Design Cycle

Professional designers follow an iterative design cycle: Define the problem → Research and analyse → Sketch and prototype → Test with users → Refine → Deliver. You'll use this cycle in your project work.

blocks.title: Design Analysis Task
blocks.intro: Choose any real website, poster, or digital document and complete a structured design analysis.
steps: Select a website, app, or printed digital product to analyse (e.g., your school website, a news site, a product poster)
Screenshot or print a section of the design
Identify and label at least 4 design elements visible in the design
Identify and explain at least 3 design principles that have been applied
Write 2–3 sentences evaluating how effectively the design serves its purpose and audience
Write one specific improvement suggestion and justify it using design language
Save or print your annotated analysis ready to share

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

question.text: Which design element refers to the empty area around and between design elements?
options: Texture
Form
Space
Line
question.explanation: Space (including white or negative space) is the empty area in a design. It improves readability and gives designs a professional, uncluttered feel.

question.text: A designer uses the same brand colour on every page of a website. Which design principle does this demonstrate?
options: Contrast
Repetition
Proximity
Balance
question.explanation: Repetition means consistently using the same visual elements (colours, fonts, shapes) throughout a design to create unity and strengthen brand identity.

question.text: Sans-serif fonts have small decorative strokes at the ends of letters.
question.explanation: False. SERIF fonts have small decorative strokes (serifs). SANS-serif means "without serifs" — they have clean, undecorated letterforms. Sans-serif fonts like Arial and Helvetica are preferred for on-screen text.

question.text: Which compositional rule divides the canvas into a 3×3 grid and places subjects at the intersection points?
options: Z-pattern
F-pattern
Rule of Thirds
Golden Ratio
question.explanation: The Rule of Thirds divides the canvas into a 3×3 grid. Placing subjects at the four intersection points (power points) creates more dynamic and visually interesting compositions than centring everything.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Cloud services like Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and Dropbox are standard in most workplaces. Knowing them well gives you an immediate edge.

question.text: A heading is large and bold, a subheading is medium weight, and body text is small and regular. Which design principle is being applied?
options: Unity
Proximity
Balance
Emphasis / Hierarchy
question.explanation: Emphasis (or visual hierarchy) directs the viewer's attention to the most important content first and guides them through information in order of importance. Size and weight differences create this hierarchy.

question.text: The Z-pattern describes how readers typically move their eyes through a text-heavy page, forming a Z shape.
question.explanation: False. Text-heavy pages typically follow an F-pattern (scanning across the top, then down the left). The Z-pattern is for pages with less text, where the eye scans across the top, diagonally to bottom-left, then across the bottom.

question.text: Which term describes the vertical space between lines of text?
options: Kerning
Tracking
Leading
Weight
question.explanation: Leading (line-height) is the vertical space between lines of text. Too tight and lines feel cramped; too loose and text feels disconnected. Kerning is space between character pairs; tracking is uniform letter-spacing across a block.

question.text: A website for young children uses large fonts, bright primary colours, and simple icons. What design concept best explains these choices?
options: Using repetition for brand consistency
Designing for purpose and audience
Applying the rule of thirds
Creating visual texture
question.explanation: Design decisions should always serve the purpose and target audience. Children need large, readable fonts, engaging bright colours, and simple visuals to navigate effectively — these are intentional audience-driven choices.

question.text: Reflect on what you've learned in this lesson. Describe how the concepts covered in "Design Concepts Quiz" could apply in a real IT workplace scenario. Give a specific example.
question.explanation: A strong answer names a specific role or industry context, connects the lesson content directly to a real task or decision, and shows understanding of why this knowledge matters in practice.

topics.title: Hardware
topics.description: Types of computer systems, internal components, input/output devices, and troubleshooting.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1001 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Client: Bunbury Regional Council. They're upgrading from paper-based workflows to digital. They need systems for three environments: a front counter, a field inspector who drives between sites, and a back-of-house data server. Your job as the junior tech: identify the right system type for each use case and justify your picks before we order anything.

blocks.title: Introducing How Computers Work
blocks.caption: Code.org — a solid foundation before we get hands-on. Watch how input, processing, output, and storage all tie together.

blocks.text: What Is a Computer System?

Any device that accepts input, processes it, stores data, and produces output is a computer system. That definition covers a lot of ground — from a Raspberry Pi sitting in a school project to a $50,000 rack server running 200 virtual machines at Bankwest.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: The hardware decision is always driven by the workload. Never spec a machine without knowing exactly what it'll be running, how many users will hit it, and whether it needs to be portable. I've seen clients burn $8,000 on a server-grade workstation for a receptionist who checks email.
The Main System Types

items.label: Desktop PC
items.description: Stays on a desk. Separate monitor, tower, keyboard, mouse. High performance per dollar, easy to upgrade. Best for: office workstations, graphic design, gaming rigs.

items.label: Laptop / Notebook
items.description: Battery-powered, all-in-one portable form factor. Slightly less raw power than equivalent desktops. Best for: mobile workers, students, field staff.

items.label: Tablet
items.description: Touchscreen, lightweight, ARM-based. No physical keyboard by default. Best for: casual browsing, point-of-sale, form collection in the field.

items.label: Smartphone
items.description: Pocket computer with cellular, GPS, camera, and sensors built in. Runs iOS or Android. Best for: communication, mobile apps, field data capture.

items.label: Server
items.description: High-spec machine (or rack of machines) designed to run 24/7 and serve requests from many users simultaneously. Best for: hosting websites, files, email, databases.

items.label: Embedded System
items.description: A processor built into a dedicated device to do one job. Examples: ATM, traffic light controller, washing machine, Cisco router firmware. Not general-purpose.

blocks.text: Side-by-Side Comparison
Servers: A Closer Look

Servers are not just fast PCs. They're built with redundancy, reliability, and uptime in mind — redundant power supplies, ECC RAM (that corrects its own memory errors), and RAID storage arrays. A Dell PowerEdge or HPE ProLiant rack server is designed to run without rebooting for years.

Cloud services — AWS, Azure, Google Cloud — are still just servers. The difference is you're renting compute time on someone else's hardware. When Leo's front-end UI hits Sloane's database, that request is bouncing through server hardware somewhere.

items.definition: A device that requests services or resources from a server.
A device that provides services, files, or compute resources to clients over a network.
A computer integrated into a device for a single dedicated purpose — not general-purpose computing.
Error-Correcting Code RAM — server-grade memory that detects and fixes single-bit errors automatically.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SYSTEM SELECTION FOR BUNBURY REGIONAL COUNCIL
blocks.text:
Return to Ticket #1001. For each of these three roles, choose the system type and write two dot-point justifications:

1. Front counter staff — greet visitors, update records in a web-based system, print forms.
2. Field building inspector — moves between 8–10 sites per day, needs to photograph and log data on-site.
3. Back-of-house — stores all council records, 30 staff access it simultaneously.

Bonus: The council asked if they could 'just use tablets for everyone to save money.' Write a one-paragraph response Sparky would give them.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A warehouse manager needs to scan barcodes, check inventory, and log deliveries while walking the floor. Which system type is MOST suitable?
options: Desktop PC — highest performance
Tablet or Smartphone — portable and purpose-built for field data capture
Server — can handle many simultaneous requests
Embedded system — designed for a dedicated task
question.explanation: Portability is the key requirement here. A tablet or smartphone can run inventory apps, scan barcodes with the camera, and be used while moving around. A desktop can't leave the desk; a server handles requests from other machines; an embedded system is locked to one task.

question.text: Cloud computing means there are no physical servers — all processing happens in the internet itself.
question.explanation: False. Cloud computing runs on physical server hardware in data centres owned by companies like Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), and Google. The 'cloud' is someone else's servers. The hardware still exists — you just don't own or manage it.

question.text: Which of the following is an example of an embedded system?
options: A Windows laptop used for graphic design
A Dell PowerEdge rack server in a data centre
The processor controlling fuel injection in a car engine
A tablet running Android apps
question.explanation: An embedded system is a processor built into a device for a single, dedicated function. The car fuel injection controller fits this perfectly — it runs one job and can't be used for general computing.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before you close this ticket, answer these three questions:

1. You've recommended a server for the council's back-of-house storage. What happens if that server's hard drive fails at 11 PM on a Friday? What hardware feature should have been spec'd to handle this?
2. Why would a tablet be a poor choice for a graphic designer who works with 4K video files all day, even though tablets run powerful ARM chips now?
3. A client says 'I just need a cheap laptop for the staff — all they do is use our web-based system.' What questions would you ask before approving that purchase?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1002 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Client: Bunbury Video Productions. Their video editor is complaining his export times have blown out from 20 minutes to over 2 hours. We've been asked to diagnose whether it's a CPU, RAM, or storage bottleneck — and quote a fix. You're shadowing me on-site today. Let's figure out what's choking this machine.

blocks.title: Inside Your Computer
blocks.caption: TED-Ed — visual breakdown of what's physically inside the machine. Watch before we dig into specs.

blocks.text: The CPU: Engine of the Machine

The CPU (Central Processing Unit) executes every instruction your software sends. It runs the fetch-decode-execute cycle billions of times per second. Modern CPUs from Intel (Core i5/i7/i9) and AMD (Ryzen 5/7/9) contain multiple cores, allowing true parallel processing.

rows: Higher = faster single-threaded tasks (spreadsheets, basic apps)
Independent processing units on one chip
More cores = better for video rendering, compiling, multitasking
Larger cache = fewer slow RAM lookups = snappier response
Effectively doubles logical core count for multi-threaded workloads
Higher TDP needs better cooling; impacts laptop battery life

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Core count beats clock speed for video work. A 3.0 GHz 8-core AMD Ryzen 7 will smoke a 4.5 GHz 2-core chip when rendering 4K footage. Clock speed matters for single-threaded tasks like gaming. Know your workload before you spec the CPU.
Memory (RAM): The Workbench

RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile — it loses everything when the power goes off. Think of it as your desk: the bigger it is, the more work you can spread out at once without shuffling things to the filing cabinet (storage). When RAM fills up, the OS starts using storage as 'virtual memory' — and that's when things grind.

blocks.title: How Computer Memory Works
blocks.caption: TED-Ed — how RAM, cache, and storage interact at a hardware level.

items.label: RAM (Primary Memory)
points: Volatile — cleared when power off
Holds currently running programs and active data
DDR4 / DDR5 — current standard
Typical ranges: 8 GB (basic), 16 GB (standard), 32–64 GB (workstation)
Directly accessed by CPU at high speed

items.label: ROM (Read-Only Memory)
points: Non-volatile — persists without power
Contains the BIOS/UEFI firmware
Initialises hardware at startup
Cannot be written to by normal software
Usually 16–128 MB on a motherboard

items.label: Cache Memory
points: Built into or adjacent to the CPU
Fastest memory in the system (nanosecond access)
L1 cache: tiny but fastest (per core)
L2/L3 cache: larger but slightly slower
Holds recently/frequently used data to avoid RAM round-trips

blocks.text: The Memory Hierarchy

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · DIAGNOSE THE VIDEO EDITOR
blocks.text:
The Bunbury Video Productions machine has these specs: Intel Core i5 (4 cores, 2.9 GHz), 8 GB DDR4 RAM, 500 GB HDD (spinning disk). The editor is working with 4K footage files averaging 40 GB each.

Tasks:
1. Identify THREE specific bottlenecks in this setup for 4K video editing. For each, name the component, explain why it's a bottleneck, and suggest a specific upgrade (include a real product name like 'Intel Core i7-13700' or 'Samsung 870 EVO SSD').
2. The client has a $1,200 upgrade budget. Rank your upgrades by impact-per-dollar and justify the order.
3. After the upgrades, which component do you predict will become the new bottleneck? Why?

items.definition: The primary processor that executes program instructions via the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
An independent processing unit within a CPU. A quad-core CPU has four units that can each process instructions simultaneously.
Volatile primary memory that holds currently running programs and active data. Cleared on power-off.
Ultra-fast memory built into or near the CPU that stores recently accessed data to avoid slower RAM lookups.
When RAM is full, the OS uses storage space (page file) as overflow memory. Much slower than actual RAM.
Measured in GHz — the number of processing cycles a CPU can complete per second per core.

question.text: A graphic designer has a PC with a fast CPU but only 8 GB of RAM. When she opens Photoshop, Illustrator, and Chrome simultaneously, the machine slows dramatically. What is the most likely cause?
options: The CPU clock speed is too low
The RAM is full and the OS is using slow virtual memory on storage
The L2 cache is too small
The ROM firmware needs updating
question.explanation: When RAM fills up, Windows or macOS moves data to a 'page file' on the hard drive or SSD — called virtual memory. Storage is orders of magnitude slower than RAM, which causes the dramatic slowdown. Adding more RAM is the fix.

question.text: ROM stores the operating system and is cleared every time the computer shuts down.
question.explanation: False. ROM contains the BIOS/UEFI firmware and is non-volatile — it persists without power. The operating system is stored on secondary storage (HDD/SSD) and loaded into RAM when the computer starts.

question.text: Explain why a CPU with more cores is better suited to video rendering than a CPU with a higher clock speed but fewer cores.
question.explanation: Video rendering is a highly parallel workload — the software can split the job across many processing threads simultaneously. More cores allow more rendering threads to run in parallel, dramatically reducing total time. A higher clock speed helps per-core tasks but does not compensate for having fewer cores on a parallel workload.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I sign off Ticket #1002, tell me:

1. The client asks 'Can I just add more cache to speed things up?' — how do you explain why they can't buy or add cache like they can buy RAM?
2. We've upgraded the RAM from 8 GB to 32 GB. The editor says it's still slow when doing very long timelines. The Task Manager shows CPU usage at 100% during exports. What does that tell you, and what's the next step?
3. Why does the memory hierarchy go from tiny-and-fast at the top to large-and-slow at the bottom? What would it take to make all memory as fast as L1 cache?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1003 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Client: Bunbury TAFE Campus IT Department. They're fitting out a new digital media lab — 20 workstations for graphic design, audio production, and video editing students. They've asked us to recommend the peripheral setup for each station. They want to know what input and output devices each workstation needs, and why. Budget per workstation: $400 for peripherals.
Input vs Output — The Basics

Every peripheral device does one of two things: it sends data INTO the computer (input) or delivers data OUT of the computer (output). Some devices do both — they're called input/output (I/O) devices.

items.label: Input Devices
points: Send data INTO the computer
Keyboard — text and command entry
Mouse / Trackpad — cursor and selection
Microphone — audio capture
Webcam / Camera — image and video capture
Scanner — digitise physical documents
Barcode reader — product identification
Graphics tablet (e.g. Wacom) — pressure-sensitive drawing

items.label: Output Devices
points: Deliver data OUT of the computer
Monitor / Display — visual output
Printer — physical document output
Speakers / Headphones — audio output
Projector — large-scale display
3D Printer — physical object output

items.label: I/O (Both)
points: Send and receive data
Touchscreen — input touch, output display
USB hub — data flows both ways
Network interface card — transmit and receive
External SSD — read and write
Headset — input mic, output audio

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Peripherals are where the bottleneck hides in creative workstations. I've seen studios drop $4,000 on a CPU and then plug in a $30 mouse and a budget monitor with terrible colour accuracy. Your monitor IS your canvas if you're in design. Don't skimp on the devices your hands and eyes touch all day.
Connection Interfaces

How a peripheral connects to the computer matters for speed, compatibility, and convenience. USB is the universal standard for most peripherals. Thunderbolt (used on Macs and high-end PCs) carries far more bandwidth than standard USB — critical for high-resolution external displays and fast storage.

rows: Still everywhere, fine for low-bandwidth peripherals
Blue port colour in most systems
Reversible connector, carries power + data + video
High-res displays, fast storage, docking stations
Common on Apple Macs and premium Windows laptops
Preferred by PC gamers and design professionals
Low latency but susceptible to interference

blocks.text: Monitors: Resolution, Refresh Rate, Colour Accuracy

For graphic design and video work, monitor specs matter more than almost any other peripheral. Resolution determines how much you can see at once. Colour accuracy (measured as DCI-P3 or sRGB coverage percentage) determines whether what you see on-screen matches what prints or publishes.

rows: Frames per second the panel can display (Hz)
60 Hz fine for design; 144+ Hz for gaming/animation
Range of colours the panel can display
Look for 99% sRGB or 90%+ DCI-P3 for design work
IPS or OLED for colour accuracy; TN is fastest but poor colour
Professional monitors (e.g. BenQ PD series) support hardware calibration

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SPEC THE TAFE MEDIA LAB
blocks.text:
You have $400 per workstation for peripherals. Each station already has the PC tower. Your job is to build the peripheral list for a graphic design/video editing student workstation.

1. Create a table listing at least 6 peripherals. For each: name the device, classify it (input/output/I/O), specify a real product (brand + model), and give the price and justification.
2. Your total must not exceed $400. Show your working.
3. Sloane's data management curriculum requires students to print reports and scan paper documents. Add the appropriate devices and adjust your budget accordingly.
4. One student has a disability requiring voice control. What additional input device would you include, and why?

items.definition: Hardware that sends data into the computer (keyboard, mouse, microphone, scanner, webcam).
Hardware that delivers data from the computer to the user (monitor, printer, speakers).
Any hardware device connected to a computer that is not the core processor or internal storage.
The dominant standard for connecting peripherals. Versions range from USB 2.0 (480 Mbps) to USB4 (40 Gbps).
The number of pixels on a display (width × height). Higher resolution = more detail and screen space.
The range of colours a display can reproduce. Measured as % coverage of sRGB or DCI-P3 colour spaces.

question.text: A music producer wants to record vocals and play them back through studio monitors. Which combination of devices does she need?
options: Two output devices: speakers and a display
An input device (microphone) and output devices (speakers and display)
Only a USB audio interface — it handles everything
An I/O device only — the audio interface counts as both input and output
question.explanation: A USB audio interface is an I/O device — it takes the microphone signal in (input) and sends audio to the monitors out (output). However, she also needs a monitor/display (output) to see her DAW software. Option B is closest to correct but the question is about the most accurate classification. An audio interface IS an I/O device.

question.text: A touchscreen is classified as an output device only, because its primary purpose is to display information.
question.explanation: False. A touchscreen is an input/output (I/O) device. It outputs visual information (display) AND receives input from the user's touch. Many definitions are both, not just one.

question.text: A video editor needs to connect an external SSD for fast file transfer. Which connection interface should she use for maximum speed?
options: USB 2.0 — it's the most compatible
Bluetooth — wireless is most convenient
Thunderbolt 3 or USB4 — provides up to 40 Gbps
HDMI — designed for high-bandwidth data transfer
question.explanation: Thunderbolt 3/4 or USB4 provides up to 40 Gbps — essential for transferring large 4K video files quickly. USB 2.0 at 480 Mbps would create a massive bottleneck. Bluetooth is far too slow. HDMI is for display signals, not storage.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
You've submitted the peripheral spec for the TAFE lab. Before I approve the order:

1. You've listed a 1080p monitor for the graphic design stations. The lecturer says students will be working on branding and print work. What specific monitor spec should you change, and why does it matter for design accuracy?
2. You recommended a USB 2.0 hub to connect all peripherals. The video students are complaining about lag when scrubbing 4K footage from their external drives. What's the problem and how do you fix it?
3. One of the recommended keyboards is wireless Bluetooth. The IT dept is worried about pairing conflicts in a lab of 20. What wired alternative would you suggest, and what connection type?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1004 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Emergency callout. Client: Bunbury Accounting Partners. A staff member's PC won't boot. Another machine is running hot and randomly shutting down. A third user says Windows is giving BSOD (Blue Screen of Death) errors once or twice a day. All three machines are needed for end-of-financial-year work starting Monday. You've got Friday afternoon to fix all three. Let's go.
The Troubleshooting Mindset

Troubleshooting is systematic, not random. You don't swap parts until something works — that's expensive and wastes time. The process is: gather symptoms, form a hypothesis, test it, document the result, escalate or resolve.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Always check the simple stuff first. Is it plugged in? Is the power strip on? Has anything changed recently — new software, moved the machine, Windows update? 80% of 'complex' faults I get called out to are something embarrassingly simple. Check before you diagnose.
The Troubleshooting Process

rows: Gather symptoms — what is and isn't working. Ask the user what changed recently.
PC won't boot. Last thing user did: installed a Windows update.
Form a hypothesis based on symptoms. Start with the most likely cause.
Theory: Update corrupted a boot file.
Try one fix at a time. Don't change multiple variables simultaneously.
Boot into Safe Mode. If it works, the theory is likely correct.
Plan the fix steps before executing. Consider risks (data loss, downtime).
Roll back the Windows update via Recovery Options.
Execute the plan. Document every action taken.
Rolled back update. Machine boots normally.
Confirm the problem is fully resolved. Record the cause and fix.
Tested for 30 minutes. Stable. Logged in ticketing system.

blocks.text: Common Hardware Faults and Causes

rows: PSU failed, power cable issue, surge damage
Check power light, test with different power cable/outlet
Dust-blocked fans, dried thermal paste, failed CPU fan
Check temperatures in BIOS or HWMonitor
Clean fans, reapply thermal paste, replace cooler
RAM fault, GPU not seated, missing component
Listen to beep pattern — refer to motherboard manual
Reseat RAM and GPU, replace faulty module
Driver conflict, RAM fault, corrupted OS files
Note the STOP code on the BSOD screen
Update/rollback driver, run memtest86, run sfc /scannow
Full RAM, fragmented HDD, malware, overheating CPU throttling
Add RAM, run disk cleanup, malware scan, clean CPU cooler
GPU/monitor cable issue, monitor power, GPU failure
Check cable connections, try different port (HDMI→DP)
Reseat GPU, replace cable, test with different monitor

blocks.text: Software Troubleshooting Tools (Windows)

items.label: Task Manager
items.description: Real-time view of CPU, RAM, disk, and network usage. Spot resource hogs instantly. Ctrl+Shift+Esc.

items.label: Event Viewer
items.description: Logs every system event including errors and crashes. BSOD details appear here after restart.

items.label: Device Manager
items.description: Lists all hardware. Yellow exclamation = driver problem. Right-click to update or roll back drivers.

items.label: sfc /scannow
items.description: System File Checker. Run in elevated CMD to scan and repair corrupted Windows system files.

items.label: Memtest86
items.description: Bootable RAM tester. Runs outside the OS to test for faulty memory modules. Run overnight.

items.label: HWiNFO / HWMonitor
items.description: Real-time hardware temperature, fan speed, and voltage monitoring. Essential for thermal faults.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · BUNBURY ACCOUNTING PARTNERS — THREE FAULTS
blocks.text:
Work through each of the three machines from Ticket #1004:

Machine A — Won't boot. Screen is black, no POST beeps, power LED is off.
Machine B — Randomly shutting down after 15–30 minutes of use. BIOS shows CPU temp hitting 95°C.
Machine C — Random BSODs. STOP code: MEMORY_MANAGEMENT. BSODs occur when running multiple browser tabs and their accounting software simultaneously.

For each machine:
1. State your initial hypothesis (most likely cause)
2. List the exact diagnostic steps you'd take in order
3. Propose the fix with the specific tool or action
4. Estimate the time and cost (parts + labour at $120/hr)

Bonus: The office manager says 'Why don't you just reinstall Windows on all three? That fixes everything, right?' Write Sparky's response.

items.definition: The self-diagnostic test a computer runs at startup before loading the OS. Beep codes indicate specific hardware faults.
A Windows critical error screen that appears when the OS encounters a fatal system error it cannot recover from. Displays a STOP code.
When a CPU automatically reduces its clock speed to prevent overheating damage. Causes sudden slowdowns under load.
Software that allows the OS to communicate with a hardware device. Outdated or corrupt drivers cause many BSOD errors.
Converts mains AC power to DC voltages the computer components require. A failing PSU causes random shutdowns and boot failures.

question.text: A PC is randomly shutting down after about 20 minutes. No error message appears. The most likely cause is:
options: A corrupted operating system file causing a silent crash
A full hard drive — the PC shuts down when storage is depleted
Thermal shutdown — the CPU is overheating and the motherboard cuts power to protect itself
A failing monitor — the display turns off and the user assumes the PC shut down
question.explanation: Random shutdowns after a consistent warm-up period are a classic sign of thermal issues. When CPU temperature exceeds the safety threshold (typically 90–100°C), the motherboard performs an emergency shutdown to prevent physical damage. Check HWMonitor or BIOS temperature readings.

question.text: You're troubleshooting a PC and want to test whether the RAM modules are faulty. Which tool is most appropriate?
options: Task Manager — check if RAM usage is at 100%
Memtest86 — boots independently of the OS to run comprehensive RAM tests
Device Manager — shows if the RAM driver has an error
sfc /scannow — scans memory and system files simultaneously
question.explanation: Memtest86 is the correct tool — it boots from a USB drive before Windows loads, running comprehensive tests on all RAM without the OS interfering. Task Manager shows usage, not faults. RAM doesn't have drivers in the Device Manager. sfc /scannow checks OS system files, not RAM.

question.text: Explain why you should change only one variable at a time when troubleshooting a hardware fault.
question.explanation: If you change multiple things at once and the problem is fixed, you don't know which change fixed it — and if a similar problem appears later, you can't replicate the solution. Changing one variable at a time lets you identify the exact cause, document a reliable fix, and avoid introducing new faults while chasing the original one.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
You've submitted your diagnostic reports for Ticket #1004. Three questions before I sign off:

1. For Machine C (MEMORY_MANAGEMENT BSOD), Memtest86 runs overnight and shows no errors. Does that rule out RAM as the cause? What else could explain the STOP code?
2. You cleaned Machine B's fans and reapplied thermal paste. The temperatures dropped from 95°C to 72°C. Is 72°C acceptable for a CPU under load? What's the safe operating range for most Intel/AMD desktop CPUs?
3. A colleague suggests you 'just image all three machines from a clean Windows backup' to save time. What are the risks of this approach, and when would it be appropriate versus when would it destroy important data?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1005 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: End of your first rotation at Bunbury Hardware Integrators. Before you move to the Networks team, Sparky needs to sign off on your hardware competency. This is the final assessment. Read each question carefully — some are scenario-based, the way they come up in real callouts.
Hardware Competency Assessment

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Hardware knowledge is foundational. You can't troubleshoot what you don't understand. Sloane can write the most efficient database queries in the world and Leo can build a beautiful UI — but if the physical layer fails, none of it matters. Know your hardware cold.

question.text: A school wants to set up a device for each classroom that automatically controls the heating, cooling, and lighting based on occupancy sensors — it needs to do nothing else. Which system type is MOST appropriate?
options: Desktop PC — provides the most computing power
Embedded system — purpose-built for a single dedicated task
Server — can manage multiple classrooms simultaneously
Tablet — easy to program and update remotely
question.explanation: An embedded system is purpose-built for a single task. Building automation controllers (like those from Schneider Electric or Honeywell) are embedded systems — they don't need general-purpose computing, just reliable, dedicated execution of their specific control logic.

question.text: Which memory type retains its contents when the computer is powered off?
options: RAM — stores all active data including when powered down
Cache — the CPU retains data between sessions
ROM — non-volatile memory that persists without power
Virtual memory — stored on disk so it always persists
question.explanation: ROM (Read-Only Memory) is non-volatile — it retains its data without power. It stores the BIOS/UEFI firmware. RAM and cache are volatile (cleared when powered off). Virtual memory is not a type of memory chip — it's OS-managed storage space used as RAM overflow.

question.text: A video editing workstation has a 6-core Intel Core i7 and 16 GB RAM. The editor frequently works on 8K footage with multiple streams. Which upgrade would provide the most significant performance improvement?
options: Replace the CPU with a higher clock speed 6-core model
Add a dedicated GPU with CUDA cores for GPU-accelerated rendering
Upgrade to 32 GB RAM
Install a faster monitor with 4K resolution
question.explanation: Modern video editing software (Premiere Pro, DaVinci Resolve) uses GPU-accelerated rendering via CUDA (NVIDIA) or OpenCL/Metal. An 8K multi-stream workflow will heavily benefit from a dedicated GPU like an NVIDIA RTX 4070. The monitor doesn't affect render speed; the CPU and RAM upgrades may help but GPU is the biggest unlock for this specific workload.

question.text: During the POST (Power-On Self-Test), a computer emits three short beeps and fails to boot. What does this most likely indicate?
options: The hard drive has failed
The operating system is corrupted
A RAM or memory fault has been detected
The CPU fan is not spinning
question.explanation: Beep codes during POST indicate specific hardware faults. Three short beeps on common BIOS implementations (AMI, Award) typically indicate a RAM error. The exact code varies by motherboard manufacturer, so always check the manual — but memory faults are among the most common POST beep error causes.

question.text: An SSD (Solid State Drive) uses spinning magnetic platters and a read/write head to store data.
question.explanation: False. An SSD stores data on NAND flash memory chips — no moving parts. Hard Disk Drives (HDDs) use spinning magnetic platters and a read/write head. SSDs are faster, more shock-resistant, and silent because of this fundamental difference.

question.text: A laptop user reports their machine is very slow when running multiple applications. Task Manager shows CPU at 25% but RAM at 98%. What is the most likely cause of the slowdown?
options: The CPU is too slow to handle the workload
The RAM is full; the OS is using virtual memory (page file on the slow SSD), causing lag
The GPU is insufficient for running multiple apps
The hard drive is nearly full, reducing storage speeds
question.explanation: CPU at 25% rules out a processing bottleneck. RAM at 98% means the system has almost no working memory left. Windows is using the page file (virtual memory on storage) for overflow — storage is many times slower than RAM, creating severe performance degradation. Adding more RAM is the fix.

question.text: Describe two differences between an HDD (Hard Disk Drive) and an SSD (Solid State Drive). For each difference, explain how it affects the use of the storage device in a real-world scenario.
question.explanation: Speed: SSDs access data in microseconds (no mechanical movement); HDDs take milliseconds due to physical platter spinning and head movement. In practice: an SSD boots Windows in 10–20 seconds vs 60+ seconds for an HDD. Durability: SSDs have no moving parts so they survive drops and vibration better; HDDs can suffer head crashes if moved while running. In practice: SSDs are essential in laptops used in the field; HDDs are more suitable in stationary desktop storage where cost per GB matters more.

question.text: Which of the following BEST describes the role of the CPU cache?
options: It stores the operating system files for fast access at startup
It provides long-term non-volatile storage like an SSD but inside the CPU
It holds recently and frequently used data so the CPU avoids slower RAM lookups
It stores backup copies of RAM contents in case of a power failure
question.explanation: CPU cache is ultra-fast memory (L1/L2/L3) built onto or near the CPU die. Its purpose is to hold recently accessed data and predicted future data so the CPU can retrieve it in nanoseconds rather than the tens-of-nanoseconds required to access RAM. It is volatile and has nothing to do with startup files or backups.

question.text: A receptionist's PC has a 12-core Intel Core i9 processor but only 8 GB of RAM. Her work involves email, Microsoft Word, and a browser-based booking system. Evaluate whether this is a well-specced machine for her role.
question.explanation: The i9 is massively over-specced for this use case — receptionist workloads are light single-threaded tasks that barely use 2 cores. The real bottleneck is the 8 GB RAM, which may struggle if multiple tabs and applications are open simultaneously. A better spec would be a mid-range CPU (i5 or Ryzen 5) with 16 GB RAM — same or lower cost, better real-world performance for this user. This wastes budget on an unnecessary processor while under-investing in the component that actually affects her daily experience.

question.text: You are called out to fix a PC that shows a black screen with the message 'No bootable device found'. The user says the PC worked fine yesterday. What is the FIRST step in your troubleshooting process?
options: Immediately reinstall Windows from a USB drive
Replace the hard drive — this message always means drive failure
Check if a USB drive or disc is inserted that the BIOS might be trying to boot from before the internal drive
Open the case and reseat all internal cables
question.explanation: The first step is always the simplest check. 'No bootable device' can be caused by a USB stick, DVD, or SD card being present that the BIOS is attempting to boot from instead of the internal drive. Remove all removable media and restart. If the problem persists, then investigate BIOS boot order, drive connection, and drive health.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I sign your rotation certificate for Hardware:

1. Looking back at all four lessons — types, CPU/memory, peripherals, and troubleshooting — which concept do you think matters most day-to-day in a real IT support role? Build your argument in 3–4 sentences.
2. A client asks you 'Why is my computer slow?' What is the single most important piece of information you need from them before you can even begin diagnosing?
3. If you had to explain the memory hierarchy (cache → RAM → storage) to a non-technical client in one analogy, what would you say?

topics.title: Impacts of Technology
topics.description: Workplace safety, privacy, digital citizenship, environmental responsibility, and changing communication.

lessons.title: Work Health and Safety in the Digital Workplace

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1001 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: The Ethics Tech Group has been asked to audit the workstation setup at a local start-up whose developers have reported rising rates of back pain and eye strain. Your task this week is to advise management on their legal WHS obligations and recommend evidence-based ergonomic improvements. Welcome to your first field assignment.

Using digital technology for extended periods without proper setup causes real, measurable harm. Work Health and Safety (WHS) law in Australia places legal duties on both employers and workers to identify and control these risks. As a future IT professional, you will be the person others look to for advice on safe digital work practices.

items.definition: The science of designing workspaces, equipment, and tasks to fit the human body and reduce the risk of injury or strain during prolonged use.
Damage to muscles, tendons, or nerves caused by repetitive movements — common in keyboard and mouse users who do not take adequate breaks.
A clinically recommended eye-rest technique: every 20 minutes, look at an object 20 feet (6 metres) away for at least 20 seconds to reduce digital eye strain.
The Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WA) — the primary legislation requiring employers to provide safe working environments, including safe computer workstation setups.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: People underestimate WHS because the injuries are slow and invisible — until they aren't. RSI doesn't announce itself with a snap; it builds quietly over months and then stops a developer's career cold. Advising on ergonomics isn't a 'soft' task. It's risk management with real human cost attached.
Ergonomic Workstation Setup
A correctly set-up workstation dramatically reduces the likelihood of physical injury during long computer sessions. The following principles apply whether working in an office, a lab, or from home.

items: Monitor: top of screen at eye level, approximately arm's length away, tilted back 10–20°
Chair: height adjusted so feet sit flat on the floor, lower back supported by lumbar cushion or adjustable backrest
Keyboard and mouse: positioned so forearms are horizontal and wrists remain straight — not bent up or down
Lighting: use indirect or diffused lighting to prevent screen glare; avoid sitting with bright windows directly behind or in front of the monitor
Documents: place beside the monitor at the same height using a document holder to minimise neck rotation
Breaks: leave the workstation for at least 5 minutes every hour and apply the 20-20-20 rule for eye health

blocks.text: Common WHS Risks in Digital Workplaces

rows: Repetitive mouse and keyboard movements, poor wrist position
Ergonomic peripherals, regular micro-breaks, proper technique
Prolonged screen use, glare, poor contrast settings, blue light
20-20-20 rule, screen brightness calibration, anti-glare filter
Poor posture, non-adjustable chair, monitor height wrong
Adjustable chair with lumbar support, monitor at eye level
Eye strain, poor lighting, dehydration, stress
Correct lighting, regular breaks, stay hydrated throughout day
Scheduled movement breaks, sit-stand desk, walking meetings

blocks.text: WHS Legal Obligations

Under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WA), employers must provide and maintain a working environment that is safe and without risks to health. This obligation extends to computer workstations, lighting, and software tools. Workers share a reciprocal duty to follow safe work practices and report hazards promptly.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · WORKSTATION AUDIT
blocks.text: You have been given photographs of four workstations at the start-up. For each one, identify: (1) at least two ergonomic problems visible in the setup, (2) the specific health risk each problem creates, and (3) one practical, low-cost fix the employer could implement immediately. Present your findings as a short briefing memo addressed to the start-up's management team.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before you finalise your workstation audit memo, answer these questions:
1. Under the WHS Act 2011, who is responsible if a worker develops RSI — the employer, the worker, or both? What does the legislation actually say?
2. If a company allows staff to work from home, do their WHS obligations still apply to the home workstation setup? Why or why not?
3. A manager argues that providing ergonomic chairs costs too much and staff 'should just take more breaks.' How would you respond using both legal and ethical reasoning?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: According to the 20-20-20 rule, what should a computer user do every 20 minutes?
options: Close their eyes for 20 seconds
Look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds
Take a 20-minute break from the screen
Blink 20 times rapidly
question.explanation: The 20-20-20 rule recommends looking at something approximately 20 feet (6 metres) away for at least 20 seconds every 20 minutes. This relaxes the eye muscles that lock in close-focus mode during prolonged screen use.

question.text: Which piece of Australian legislation primarily governs employer obligations regarding safe computer workstations?
options: Privacy Act 1988
Copyright Act 1968
Work Health and Safety Act 2011
Australian Consumer Law
question.explanation: The Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WA) requires employers to provide and maintain a safe working environment, which includes ergonomically appropriate computer workstations. The Privacy Act and Copyright Act cover different aspects of digital work.

question.text: A graphic designer works 8-hour days at a desktop computer and has started experiencing wrist pain. Identify two ergonomic changes to her workstation that could reduce this risk, and explain the WHS obligation her employer has in this situation.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark each for two valid ergonomic changes (e.g., ergonomic mouse/keyboard, wrist rest, adjusted chair height, scheduled micro-breaks). Award 2 marks for correctly identifying the employer's WHS Act 2011 obligation to provide a safe environment and take reasonable steps to address identified hazards.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1002 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: A local school is building a student management portal. They want to collect names, photos, health records, and GPS check-in data for every student. Before a single line of code goes live, they need a Privacy Act compliance review. That review is your job. Sloane's data systems need to comply with the Privacy Act 1988 before they go live — and right now, they're a long way from compliant.

Every time you sign up for an app, use a school portal, or buy something online, personal information is collected about you. The Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) is Australia's primary law protecting that information. As IT professionals, we don't just need to know this law — we need to build systems that comply with it from day one.

items.definition: Any information or opinion about an identified individual, or an individual who is reasonably identifiable — including name, address, photo, health records, and financial details (Privacy Act 1988).
A subset of personal information that attracts higher legal protection: health, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, sexual orientation, and biometric data.
An incident where personal information is accessed, disclosed, or lost without authorisation — triggering mandatory notification requirements under the Notifiable Data Breaches scheme.
The 13 principles in Schedule 1 of the Privacy Act 1988 that govern how organisations collect, use, store, and disclose personal information.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: The Privacy Act is not optional reading. Any organisation with an annual turnover above $3 million — and many smaller ones — must comply. But beyond legal compliance, privacy is a human right. When we collect data about people, we take on a responsibility to protect it as if it were our own. The moment that obligation becomes bureaucratic box-ticking, people get hurt.
What the Privacy Act 1988 Requires

The Australian Privacy Principles establish 13 obligations for any organisation covered by the Act. The most critical for IT developers and administrators are: collecting only the minimum information necessary; using it only for the purpose it was collected; storing it securely; and allowing individuals to access and correct their own records.

rows: Only collect personal information you genuinely need
Collecting GPS location when only a postcode is required
Only use data for the purpose it was originally collected for
Selling customer email addresses to a third-party advertiser
Store personal information securely and protect it from misuse
Storing passwords in plain text in an unencrypted database
Allow individuals to see and correct their own data
Refusing a customer's request to update an incorrect address
Notify the OAIC and affected individuals of eligible breaches
Discovering a breach but quietly fixing it without notification

blocks.text: Protecting Your Own Privacy Online

items: Use strong, unique passwords for each account and enable multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Read privacy policies before entering personal information — look for what data is collected and how it is shared
Limit the personal information you share on social media (birthdates, addresses, phone numbers)
Use privacy settings to restrict who can see your posts and personal details
Be cautious of apps that request permissions beyond what their function requires (e.g., a torch app requesting contacts)

blocks.title: Scenario: The School Portal
blocks.scenario: Harmony High is building a student portal that will collect: full name, home address, photo, daily GPS check-in location, health records, and exam results. The portal will be accessible to all teaching staff and will share anonymised data with a commercial research company to 'improve education outcomes.' A Year 11 student has asked to see what data the school holds about her and has it corrected.

questions.question: Which of the data items collected would be classified as 'sensitive information' under the Privacy Act 1988? Why does this classification matter?
Is sharing data with the commercial research company permitted under the Australian Privacy Principles? What would need to be true for this sharing to be lawful?
The school must respond to the student's request to access and correct her data. What are their obligations under the Privacy Act 1988, and what outcome should the student receive?

blocks.title: Spot the Privacy Breach
blocks.instruction: Review these two emails received by a student. Identify which one is a privacy threat and what the red flags are.

emails.subject: URGENT: Verify your student account to avoid suspension
emails.body:
Dear Student,

Your account at Harmony High School Portal requires immediate verification. Please click the link below and enter your full name, date of birth, student ID, and home address to confirm your identity.

FAILURE TO VERIFY WITHIN 24 HOURS WILL RESULT IN ACCOUNT SUSPENSION.

Click here: http://myschoolportal-verify.xyz/login

Harmony High IT Department

redFlags.detail: Uses a capital 'I' instead of lowercase 'l' in 'portaI' — a look-alike domain designed to deceive
Links to an external .xyz domain, not the school's official domain — a classic phishing redirect
'24 hours or suspension' is a pressure tactic used to bypass critical thinking
Legitimate school portals already hold student data — they would never ask you to re-enter it via email

emails.subject: Scheduled maintenance: portal unavailable Saturday 8am–10am
emails.body:
Hi Students,

Please be advised that the Harmony High student portal will be unavailable for scheduled maintenance this Saturday from 8:00am to 10:00am.

No action is required from you. Your data will not be affected.

If you have questions, please contact the IT Help Desk via the school website.

Thanks,
Harmony High IT Support

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before we sign off on the privacy compliance review:
1. The portal collects GPS location data. Under which Australian Privacy Principle must the school justify this collection — and what test must they meet?
2. A staff member accidentally emails a student's health record to the wrong parent. Does this trigger the Notifiable Data Breaches scheme? What factors determine this?
3. The research company says all data is 'anonymised' before they receive it. Is anonymised data automatically outside the scope of the Privacy Act? What are the risks of over-relying on anonymisation?

question.text: Under the Privacy Act 1988, which of the following best describes 'sensitive information'?
options: Any information that the individual considers private
Information that is expensive or difficult to collect
Health, racial origin, religious beliefs, and biometric data — attracting stronger legal protection
Financial information held by banks
question.explanation: Sensitive information is defined in the Privacy Act 1988 and includes health information, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, sexual orientation, and biometric data. It attracts stricter obligations around collection and use than ordinary personal information.

question.text: Under the Privacy Act 1988, an organisation can use personal information collected for one purpose for any other purpose it chooses, as long as the data is kept secure.
question.explanation: False. The Australian Privacy Principles restrict the use of personal information to the purpose for which it was originally collected. Using it for a different purpose generally requires fresh consent from the individual, unless a specific exception applies.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1003 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: The Ethics Tech Group has received a formal complaint from a school about a coordinated cyberbullying campaign targeting a Year 11 student across three platforms. You have been asked to brief the school's leadership on: what constitutes cyberbullying under Australian law, what platforms are required to do, and what actions the school can take. This is a legal, ethical, and reputational issue — handle it with precision.

Being online isn't just a technical activity — it's a social and civic one. Digital citizenship describes the responsible, ethical, and safe use of technology. It includes how we treat others online, the content we share, and the long-term implications of the digital footprint we leave behind. These are not abstract values; they are increasingly backed by enforceable law.

items.definition: The responsible, ethical, and safe use of technology — including respectful online communication, protecting personal information, and understanding the legal consequences of online behaviour.

Using digital technology to repeatedly harass, intimidate, threaten, or humiliate another person. In Australia, serious cyberbullying of a child is a criminal offence under the Online Safety Act 2021.
The permanent trail of data left by online activity — including posts, messages, searches, and account sign-ups. Digital footprints can be accessed by employers, universities, and law enforcement.
Publishing false information that damages a person's reputation. Online posts, comments, and reviews can constitute defamation under Australian law — individuals can be personally sued.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: The internet has a longer memory than you do. Students have lost university placements and job offers because of posts made years earlier. The question I ask organisations — and that I want you to ask yourselves — is: would you be comfortable if this post, this comment, this message appeared on the front page of a newspaper? If not, don't publish it. The digital world doesn't have an 'unsend' button that actually works.
Forms of Cyberbullying

rows: Repeated threatening or abusive messages sent directly to a person
Sending dozens of insulting messages to a classmate's phone each night
Deliberately and repeatedly excluding someone from online groups or activities
Removing a student from every group chat and telling others not to include them
Sharing someone's private information or secrets publicly without consent
Posting someone's home address or revealing private health information online
Creating fake accounts to post harmful content in someone else's name
Making a fake Instagram account using a classmate's photo and posting offensive content
Using technology to repeatedly monitor and intimidate a specific person
Tracking someone's location via social media check-ins and showing up uninvited
Posting hostile, aggressive messages in public online spaces to provoke
Writing vicious public comments attacking a student on their social media page

blocks.text: Legal Framework: Online Conduct in Australia

rows: Cyberbullying of children; removal of harmful content; platform obligations
eSafety Commissioner can order removal of content; platforms face fines
Using a carriage service (internet/phone) to menace, harass, or offend
Up to 3 years imprisonment for using the internet to harass
Publishing false statements that damage a person's reputation
Civil lawsuits; court orders to remove content; damages payments
Sharing personal information about others without consent
Complaints to the OAIC; financial penalties for organisations

blocks.text: Managing Your Digital Footprint

items: Google yourself regularly to understand what information is publicly visible about you
Review and tighten privacy settings on all social media platforms
Think before posting: consider whether future employers, universities, or family members would see this
Avoid sharing others' personal information without explicit consent
Report harmful content to platform moderators and, where appropriate, to the eSafety Commissioner (esafety.gov.au)

blocks.title: Spot the Cyberbullying
blocks.instruction: Read these two messages. Identify which one constitutes cyberbullying under the Online Safety Act 2021 and explain the specific behaviours that make it harmful and potentially unlawful.

emails.subject: Public post on a school Facebook group
emails.body: Everyone look at this loser [name]'s photo lmao. Can't believe this person thinks they're good at anything. We all know what you did at the camp. Keep your mouth shut or I'll post the whole story. FYI I've added your home address so people know where to find you. Everyone share this 😂

redFlags.detail: 'Keep your mouth shut or I'll post the whole story' is an explicit threat — a criminal offence under the Criminal Code Act 1995
Publishing someone's home address without consent is a serious privacy violation and a form of cyberbullying
Asking others to 'share this' turns individual harassment into coordinated group bullying — covered by the Online Safety Act 2021
Hiding behind anonymity does not provide legal protection — investigators can and do trace anonymous accounts

emails.subject: Hey everyone
emails.body: Hey team — just a reminder that our fundraiser meeting has moved to Thursday at lunch. Room B14. If you can't make it, just let me know and I'll send you the notes. Cheers!

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · DIGITAL CITIZENSHIP POLICY BRIEF
blocks.text: A local sporting club has asked you to draft a 'Digital Conduct Policy' for its members, covering online communications, social media, and group chats. Your policy must: (1) define acceptable and unacceptable online conduct in plain language; (2) reference at least two relevant Australian laws; (3) specify what will happen if a member breaches the policy; and (4) include a reporting mechanism for members experiencing online harassment. Keep it to one page — policies that are too long don't get read.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before your digital conduct policy goes to the club:
1. The policy says members who violate it will be 'banned from social media.' Can a club legally ban a member from using social media? What can the club actually enforce?
2. A member is being harassed via private messages, not public posts. Does the Online Safety Act 2021 still apply to private communications? Who has jurisdiction?
3. Your policy requires members to report cyberbullying to the club president. What if the club president is the person doing the bullying? How should your policy account for this?

question.text: Which Australian body has the power to order social media platforms to remove cyberbullying content targeting children?
options: The Australian Federal Police
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC)
The eSafety Commissioner
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC)
question.explanation: The eSafety Commissioner has powers under the Online Safety Act 2021 to issue removal notices to social media platforms requiring them to take down cyberbullying content. The OAIC handles privacy complaints, and the AFP handles criminal investigations.

question.text: Explain what a 'digital footprint' is and describe two specific ways a young person's digital footprint could negatively affect their future employment prospects.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark for a correct definition of digital footprint (the permanent trail of data left by online activity). Award 1 mark each for two valid examples, such as: offensive social media posts found by a prospective employer; discriminatory comments that contradict an organisation's values; evidence of dishonesty contradicting a job application claim. Award up to 4 marks total.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #1004 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: A regional government office is upgrading 200 computers. They plan to throw the old ones in a general waste skip. Before they do, we need to stop them — and explain why this is both illegal and ethically indefensible. Your brief must cover the environmental harm, the data security risk, and the legal disposal obligations. Sparky's network upgrade is technically sound, but where does the hardware go? That's our problem to solve.

Electronic waste — or e-waste — is the fastest-growing solid waste stream in the world. Australia generates approximately 554,000 tonnes of e-waste per year, much of which is still disposed of in landfill. As IT professionals, we have an ethical and legal responsibility for what happens to hardware when it reaches end of life.

items.definition: Discarded electrical and electronic equipment including computers, phones, televisions, batteries, and peripherals — which contain hazardous materials that can leach into soil and water if landfilled.
Australian legislation that creates shared responsibility for the environmental impact of products across their entire lifecycle, including end-of-life disposal.

items.term: National Television and Computer Recycling Scheme (NTCRS)
items.definition: An Australian government program that provides free drop-off recycling for televisions and computers, funded by product importers and manufacturers.
The deliberate, permanent, and irreversible removal of data from storage media — going beyond simple deletion to ensure data cannot be recovered by any method.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
