1. An employer says 'managing your screen time is your own responsibility — we just supply the equipment.' Under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WA), is this a legally defensible position? What specific obligation does the employer have?
2. A developer works from home and reports developing RSI. Does the employer's WHS duty of care extend to the home office? What does the legislation say?
3. A student argues that blue light glasses will solve all their sleep issues caused by late-night phone use. Based on the evidence, is this accurate? What additional changes would the evidence actually support?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: According to the 20-20-20 rule for reducing digital eye strain, what should you do every 20 minutes?
options: Close your eyes for 20 seconds
Look at something 20 metres away for 20 seconds
Take a 20-minute screen break
Reduce screen brightness by 20%
question.explanation: The 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes, look at something at least 20 feet (approximately 6 metres) away for at least 20 seconds. This allows the ciliary muscles that control eye focus to relax after prolonged near-focus work.

question.text: Blue light emitted by digital screens can disrupt sleep by suppressing melatonin production.
question.explanation: True. Research shows that blue-wavelength light emitted by screens suppresses melatonin — the hormone that signals the brain to initiate sleep — making it harder to fall asleep and reducing sleep quality. This is why reducing screen use 1 hour before bed is widely recommended.

question.text: A Year 11 student reports that after a full school day on computers, they experience headaches, difficulty focusing on homework, and often scroll social media until midnight. (a) Identify two physical health risks they are experiencing and name one specific prevention strategy for each. (b) Identify one mental health risk their behaviour pattern suggests. (c) What change would have the greatest impact on their sleep quality? Explain using evidence.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks (1 each) for two physical risks with prevention: e.g., digital eye strain → 20-20-20 rule / headaches → scheduled breaks and hydration / RSI → ergonomic setup. Award 1 mark for identifying a mental health risk: FOMO, social media anxiety, or sleep disruption from digital addiction patterns. Award 2 marks: stopping screen use at least 1 hour before sleep (not just switching to phone) is the highest-impact change — blue light suppresses melatonin, and the variable reward schedule of social media creates cognitive arousal that delays sleep onset.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3006 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN ASSESSMENT: This is your end-of-topic review. The Regional Ethics Tech Group evaluates every intern on their ability to apply legal and ethical knowledge to real scenarios — not just recall definitions. Work through these questions carefully. Dr. Finch will review your responses before you progress to the next assignment.

blocks.heading: 📋 Unit 2 — Impacts of Technology: What You've Covered
blocks.text:
This topic covered five key areas:
1. Types of Computer Crime — cybercrime categories, Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth)
2. Copyright and IP — Copyright Act 1968 (Cth), Creative Commons, moral rights
3. The Spam Act 2003 — consent, identify, unsubscribe rules, ACMA enforcement
4. Digital Citizenship — cyberbullying types, eSafety Commissioner, online conduct
5. Health Impacts — physical risks (RSI, eye strain, posture) and mental health risks (anxiety, sleep disruption, FOMO)

question.text: Which Australian legislation primarily governs commercial email and SMS messages sent for business purposes?
options: Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
Spam Act 2003 (Cth)
Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth)
Online Safety Act 2021 (Cth)
question.explanation: The Spam Act 2003 (Cth) governs commercial electronic messages including email and SMS. It requires senders to have consent, identify themselves, and provide an unsubscribe mechanism.

question.text: Under the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth), a creator must register their work with a government office for copyright protection to apply.
question.explanation: False. Copyright protection is automatic under Australian law — it applies the moment an original work is created and recorded, with no registration required.

question.text: A business receives 300 unsubscribe requests on a Monday. Under the Spam Act 2003 (Cth), by when must these be processed?
options: The same day
Within 2 business days
Within 5 business days
Within 10 business days
question.explanation: The Spam Act 2003 (Cth) requires unsubscribe requests to be processed within 5 business days. Failure to do so is a violation that ACMA can investigate and fine.

question.text: Malicious software that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key is called ransomware.
question.explanation: True. Ransomware is a type of malware that encrypts files on the victim's system, then demands payment (typically in cryptocurrency) in exchange for the decryption key.

question.text: Which Creative Commons licence allows free commercial use but requires the user to release any adaptations under the same licence?
options: CC BY
CC BY-SA
CC BY-NC
CC BY-ND
question.explanation: CC BY-SA (Attribution ShareAlike) allows commercial use and adaptations but requires any derivative works to be released under the same CC BY-SA licence — this is the 'ShareAlike' condition.

question.text: The eSafety Commissioner has the power to order social media platforms to remove cyberbullying content targeting Australians.
question.explanation: True. The Online Safety Act 2021 (Cth) established the eSafety Commissioner with powers to investigate complaints and issue removal notices to platforms for cyberbullying material, CSAM, and other harmful content.

question.text: A developer suffers from wrist pain after years of keyboard use. This condition is most accurately described as:
options: Digital eye strain
Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)
Sedentary behaviour
Tension headache
question.explanation: Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI) refers to damage to muscles, tendons, or nerves caused by repetitive movements — extremely common in keyboard and mouse users. Ergonomic equipment and regular breaks are the primary preventions.

question.text: Moral rights under the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth) can be sold or transferred to another person.
question.explanation: False. Moral rights — including the right of attribution and the right of integrity — cannot be transferred or waived under the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth). They remain with the creator permanently, even if economic copyright is sold.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4201 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 7 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

The Launchpad is pitching to Bunbury City Council next Tuesday. 10 slides, 8 minutes, decision-makers who have seen a thousand boring PowerPoints. Leo's done the visual design, Sloane's prepared the data — now we need someone who actually knows how to USE the tools. Before you open a single blank slide, you need to know what every feature does and when to reach for it.

Presentation software is not just about putting bullet points on a screen — it is about controlling how information lands in the room. PowerPoint and Google Slides share a core feature set, but knowing which features actually matter in a professional context separates the interns who slow the team down from the ones who speed it up.

items.definition: A template that controls the default fonts, colours, layouts, and logo placement across every slide in a presentation. Editing the master changes all slides simultaneously.
The animation that plays between slides. In professional contexts, these should be minimal — a simple fade or cut. Spinning cubes and dissolve effects are the mark of an amateur.
Movement applied to individual elements on a slide — text that fades in, charts that build bar by bar. Used sparingly, they guide the audience's attention. Overused, they destroy credibility.
A dual-screen mode where the speaker sees their speaker notes and the next slide on their laptop, while the audience sees only the current slide on the projector.
A text field below each slide where the presenter stores their script, talking points, and reminders. These are invisible to the audience during presentation.

blocks.text: The Feature Set That Actually Matters

rows: Sets global design: fonts, colours, backgrounds, logos
Apply once at the start — never format slides individually
Pre-built arrangements of placeholders (title, content, two-column)
Choose the right layout per slide — don't manually position everything
One consistent transition max (Fade or None). Avoid Fly, Zoom, Cube.
Movement of individual elements on a slide
Use Appear or Fade to reveal data points one at a time during Q&A
Speaker sees notes + next slide; audience sees current slide only
Essential for any live presentation — prevents turning your back on the room
Write full sentences, not dot points — you will forget the dot points under pressure
Live charts linked to Excel/Sheets data
Insert → Chart from Sheets; updates automatically when data changes
Clickable links to external resources or other slides
Link to live demos, reports, or jump to an appendix slide during Q&A
Always export to PDF before a presentation — PowerPoint fonts break on unfamiliar machines

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I have watched animated text, spinning logos, and comic sans fonts cost clients the contract. Animations are not features — they are a distraction tax your audience pays while waiting for the actual information. One font. One consistent colour. Minimal transitions. The content wins the room, not the pyrotechnics.
PowerPoint vs Google Slides

items.label: Microsoft PowerPoint
points: Industry standard for formal client and government presentations
Superior animation control and Morph transition for smooth data reveals
Offline — works without internet during the presentation
Better for complex designs and precise positioning
Export to .pptx — universally accepted by clients

items.label: Google Slides
points: Real-time collaboration — multiple team members edit simultaneously
Auto-saved in Drive, accessible from any device
Easy to share with clients for review without file attachments
Ideal for team-built decks in agile sprints
Fewer animation options but sufficient for most business decks

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · BUILD YOUR PITCH STRUCTURE
blocks.text:
Open PowerPoint or Google Slides. Apply the built-in 'Wisp' or 'Ion' theme (or any professional theme). Create 5 slides with these layouts:
1. Title slide — presentation title + your name + date
2. Problem slide — one sentence, one supporting stat
3. Solution slide — three bullet points, one per click (add Appear animations)
4. Data slide — insert a table with 3 rows and 3 columns of made-up project metrics
5. Next steps slide — numbered list, 3 items

Export to PDF when done. The PDF must look identical to the slides.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
1. Your colleague has used 6 different fonts, 4 transitions (Cube, Fly, Ripple, Zoom), and 12 animations per slide on the council pitch deck. You are the only one who can save this. What specific changes do you make, and why does each matter?
2. You are presenting from your own laptop, but the projector only shows one screen. You cannot see your speaker notes. What feature were you supposed to set up, and how do you fix this without restarting?
3. You finish a presentation and the Council asks for a copy 'in case the projector fails next time.' Do you send the .pptx file or a PDF? Explain the risk you are managing.

question.text: A designer changes the company logo on the Slide Master of a 40-slide PowerPoint. How many slides need to be manually updated?
options: All 40 slides
Only slides that use the default layout
None — the change propagates to all slides automatically
Only the title slide
question.explanation: Changes to the Slide Master apply automatically to every slide that uses the master's layout. This is the primary purpose of the Slide Master — global consistency without manual repetition.

question.text: Exporting a presentation to PDF before delivery is a professional best practice because it eliminates font substitution issues on unfamiliar computers.
question.explanation: True. PowerPoint files rely on fonts being installed on the presenting computer. If the target machine lacks your fonts, PowerPoint substitutes defaults, breaking the design. A PDF embeds all fonts and renders identically on any device.

question.text: A junior analyst builds a 20-slide deck for a client pitch without using the Slide Master, formatting each slide individually. On the day of the pitch, the client asks for the company logo to be replaced with the new version and the colour scheme changed from blue to green. Explain (a) why this is a significant problem given how the deck was built, and (b) how using the Slide Master from the start would have prevented this.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: Without a Slide Master, the logo and colour must be manually updated on all 20 slides — tedious, error-prone, and time-consuming immediately before a high-stakes presentation. Award 2 marks: With a Slide Master, the logo appears in one place (the master) and the colour scheme is part of the theme — changing either updates all slides instantly, with no risk of missing a slide.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4202 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 7 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

Today you present your first mini-report to the full Launchpad team. 8 slides, 5 minutes, then 3 minutes of questions. This is not a class — Sloane will interrupt if the data is wrong, Leo will notice if the design is off, and I will stop you if you are reading off the screen. Prepare the deck, know the content, and deliver like you have done this before.
Slide Design Principles That Actually Work

items: 6×6 rule: No more than 6 bullet points per slide, no more than 6 words per bullet. Your slides are visual cues, not a script for the audience to read instead of listening to you.
One idea per slide: If you need to scroll text or use a tiny font to fit everything, you have two slides masquerading as one.
High contrast: White or light text on dark backgrounds, or dark text on light backgrounds. Grey on grey is invisible under projector lighting.
Font hierarchy: Two fonts maximum — one for headings (bold, larger), one for body (lighter, smaller). Never mix decorative and formal fonts in a business deck.
White space is not wasted space: Overcrowded slides make the audience feel trapped. Leave breathing room — it makes each element feel more important, not less.
Visuals over bullet points: A well-chosen image or chart communicates faster than five dot points. If you can show it, show it.

blocks.text: Embedding Charts from Data Sources

A chart in a presentation should always be linked to its data source when possible. In Google Slides, charts inserted from Google Sheets maintain a live link — when Sloane updates the data, a click of 'Update' refreshes the chart in your slides. In PowerPoint, you can embed an Excel chart that updates when the Excel file is edited. This eliminates the risk of showing stale data to a client.

blocks.title: Build an 8-Slide Report Deck
blocks.intro: Follow these steps to build a professionally structured presentation with embedded data and proper speaker notes.
steps: Open Google Slides or PowerPoint and select a professional theme (avoid templates with heavy decorative borders or cartoon elements).
Set up the Slide Master first: Ensure the company name appears in the footer and the colour palette matches the theme.
Slide 1 (Title): Presentation title, your name, date. Use the title layout — do not manually type in the body area.
Slide 2 (Problem / Context): One sentence describing the issue. One statistic to support it. No more.
Slide 3 (Key Finding 1): A heading and one chart. In Google Slides: Insert → Chart → from Sheets (link to a Google Sheet you've created with 3 rows of data).
Slide 4 (Key Finding 2): A heading and one table with 3 columns and 4 rows.
Slides 5–6 (Analysis): Two slides with one key point each. Apply bullet-point animations using Appear — so points reveal one at a time during your presentation.
Slide 7 (Recommendation): Three numbered actions. Keep each to one line.
Slide 8 (Thank You / Questions): Your name and contact. Nothing else.
Add speaker notes to every slide — write full sentences, not shorthand.
Export to PDF and verify it looks identical to the slides view.

blocks.text: Delivering Effectively

rows: Spend 70% of time looking at the audience, 30% at the screen
Reading word-for-word from slides — the audience can read faster than you speak
Aim for 120–140 words per minute; pause between key points
Rushing through slides when nervous; skipping pauses
Use sparingly to highlight a specific data point; put it down
Waving the laser pointer constantly or drawing circles
Share the specific window, not your whole desktop
Audience sees your taskbar, notifications, and private files
Repeat the question for the room, then answer directly
Guessing or over-explaining when you don't know the answer

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The number one mistake I see from junior presenters: they turn their back on the room to look at the screen. Your audience is in front of you. The screen is a prop. Know your content well enough that the slides are a reminder, not a teleprompter.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PEER PRESENT
blocks.text: Once your 8-slide deck is complete, present slides 2–4 to a partner (2 minutes). Your partner's job: count how many times you look at the screen instead of at them, and note one specific thing you did well. Swap and repeat. The feedback is not optional — write down what your partner said and what you will change for the real presentation.

question.text: According to the 6×6 rule, a slide should contain no more than:
options: 6 slides per presentation and 6 words per title
6 bullet points per slide and 6 words per bullet
6 images per slide and 6 colour choices
6 fonts per slide and 6 animations per transition
question.explanation: The 6×6 rule limits slides to 6 bullet points maximum, with no more than 6 words per bullet. This keeps slides as visual cues rather than scripts, forcing the presenter to deliver the detail verbally.

question.text: A presenter is showing a 15-slide deck via screen share in Microsoft Teams. The best practice is to share:
options: The entire desktop so the audience can see everything
Only the PowerPoint window to avoid exposing private files
A PDF version because Teams does not support PowerPoint
The slideshow in a separate browser tab
question.explanation: When screen sharing, always share only the specific application window — not your entire desktop. Sharing the desktop risks exposing private files, notifications, taskbar items, and other applications.

question.text: During a client presentation, an audience member asks a question that you are not sure how to answer. Describe the professional approach to handling this situation, and explain why making up an answer is a risk to more than just your personal credibility.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark: the professional response is to acknowledge the question honestly — 'That's a great question and I want to make sure I give you accurate information. I'll follow up with a written response by end of week.' Award 2 marks: fabricating an answer risks providing incorrect information to a decision-maker who may act on it; damages the organisation's reputation if the error is discovered; and destroys personal credibility in a professional context where trust is the foundation of every relationship.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4203 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 8 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

The client needs a 60-second voiceover for their product explainer video. They have sent us the script. We have the microphone. What we do not have yet is someone who knows how to record, clean up, and export a professional-sounding audio file. You are that person now. Audacity is installed on your machine. Learn it. Deliver it.

Audio software is used across professional IT contexts — voiceovers for client videos, podcast content, accessibility features (text-to-speech alternatives), and screen recordings with narration. Audacity is the industry-standard free tool. Understanding how it works — and how audio itself works — is the difference between delivering a professional clip and submitting a file that sounds like it was recorded in a bathroom.

items.definition: How many times per second audio is captured (measured in Hz). 44,100 Hz (44.1 kHz) is CD quality and the standard for voiceovers and music. Higher rates use more storage.
The number of bits used to encode each audio sample — determines dynamic range. 16-bit is CD standard; 24-bit is used in professional recording for extra headroom.
The level of background noise present even when nothing is being recorded — hum, hiss, fan noise. A low noise floor is essential for professional audio.
The amplification applied to an audio signal. Setting gain too high causes clipping (distortion); too low results in a quiet recording buried in noise.
A process that automatically raises or lowers the peak level of a recording to a target volume without distorting the waveform.

blocks.text: Core Audacity Workflow

items: Set your input device: Select the correct microphone from the device dropdown before recording.
Set sample rate to 44,100 Hz and bit depth to 16-bit for standard voiceover work.
Do a test recording — speak at normal volume and check the waveform stays between -12 dB and -6 dB. If it regularly hits 0 dB (red), reduce your gain.
Record your take. If you stumble, pause and repeat the sentence — do not stop the recording.
Apply Noise Reduction: Effect → Noise Reduction. First, select 2 seconds of silence to capture the noise profile, then apply to the whole track.
Trim silence from the start and end using the Selection tool and Edit → Delete.
Apply Fade In (0.5 sec) at the start and Fade Out (0.5 sec) at the end using Effect → Fading.
Normalise to -1 dB: Effect → Normalize. This brings volume to a consistent level.
Export: File → Export Audio. Choose MP3 for web/video delivery (128 kbps minimum), WAV for archival.

blocks.text: Audio File Formats Compared

rows: Good at 192 kbps+, audible artefacts at low bitrates
Better than MP3 at same file size

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Always record in WAV, export in MP3. Never edit an MP3 file — every time you export a lossy file it degrades further. Your master is WAV. Your delivery is MP3. The distinction matters when the client comes back six months later needing a re-edit.

blocks.title: Record, Clean, and Export a 30-Second Voiceover
blocks.intro: Complete this workflow to produce a broadcast-quality 30-second voiceover clip.
steps: Open Audacity. Set sample rate (bottom left) to 44100 Hz.
Select your USB or headset microphone from the input device dropdown.

Press Record (R) and speak the following 30-second script at a measured pace: 'Welcome to the Bunbury Tech Launchpad. We help local businesses solve real problems with smart, practical technology solutions. Whether you need a website, a data system, or a digital strategy, our team delivers on time and on brief. Contact us today to book a free consultation.'
Stop the recording. Check that the waveform peaks sit between -12 dB and -6 dB consistently.
Select 1–2 seconds of silence at the start of the recording (before you spoke). Go to Effect → Noise Reduction → Get Noise Profile.
Select All (Ctrl+A). Return to Effect → Noise Reduction and click OK to apply.
Trim the silence from the beginning and end of the track.
Apply Effect → Normalize, set peak to -1.0 dB, click OK.
Go to File → Export Audio → Export as MP3 at 192 kbps. Name the file launchpad-voiceover.mp3.
Play back the exported file in a media player and compare to the raw recording. Note the difference in background noise.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · CLEAN VS DIRTY AUDIO
blocks.text:
Record two versions of the same 10-second script:
- Version A: Recorded normally, no post-processing
- Version B: Recorded with laptop fan running loudly nearby, then cleaned with Noise Reduction

Compare the two exported MP3 files. Can you hear the difference? Write 2 sentences describing what changed between the raw and cleaned version, and one scenario where noise reduction would NOT be enough to save the recording.

question.text: A voiceover is recorded in WAV format and will be delivered as part of a web video. What is the correct export format and minimum recommended bitrate?
options: WAV at 44,100 Hz
MP3 at 64 kbps
MP3 at 128–192 kbps
OGG at 320 kbps
question.explanation: MP3 at 128–192 kbps is the standard for web delivery voiceovers — small enough for streaming, with acceptable quality. 64 kbps produces audible compression artefacts. WAV files are too large for web delivery. OGG is less universally supported by video editors.

question.text: Editing an MP3 file and exporting it as another MP3 degrades the audio quality because lossy compression is applied a second time.
question.explanation: True. Every time a lossy format like MP3 is compressed, information is discarded. Editing an MP3 and re-exporting as MP3 applies a second round of compression on top of the first, introducing additional artefacts. Always work from WAV masters.

question.text: A client receives their voiceover and complains that there is a constant low hum throughout the clip, and the voice sounds too quiet. (a) Identify the two issues by their correct technical terms. (b) Describe the specific Audacity steps you would take to fix each issue on the WAV master file.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: (1) High noise floor / background noise — fixed with Effect → Noise Reduction (capture profile from silence, apply to track). (2) Low gain / quiet recording — fixed with Effect → Normalise (set peak to -1 dB) or Effect → Amplify. Award 2 marks for correct sequencing: apply noise reduction BEFORE normalising — normalising amplifies the noise floor along with the signal if done first.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4204 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 9 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

The client is launching their website Friday. Leo has approved the product design but we need three product images prepared: background removed, resized to 800×800px, exported as WebP for the web and PNG for print. The client sent us JPEGs shot on a phone with cluttered backgrounds. Photopea is installed — go.

items.definition: An image made of a fixed grid of pixels. Zooming in shows individual pixels — the image becomes 'pixelated'. Formats: JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF.
An image defined by mathematical paths and shapes, not pixels. Scales to any size without quality loss. Formats: SVG, AI, EPS. Used for logos and icons.
A transparent sheet stacked on top of other sheets in image editing software. Editing one layer does not affect others — essential for non-destructive editing.
The resolution of a digital image. 72 PPI is standard for screen display; 300 PPI is the minimum for professional print quality.
Lossless compression (PNG, WebP lossless) preserves every pixel of data. Lossy compression (JPEG, WebP lossy) discards some data to achieve smaller file sizes — quality degrades with each save.

blocks.text: Image File Formats — When to Use Each

rows: Logos, text overlays, images that need re-editing
Logos, product images on transparent backgrounds, screenshots
Large photos where file size matters
Web delivery — 25–35% smaller than JPEG/PNG with similar quality
Print — not all print services accept WebP
Logos, icons, illustrations that must scale
Photographs — SVG cannot represent photographic detail

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I have received logos as JPEGs from clients and the results are always the same: white box around the logo that looks terrible on anything other than a white background. Always ask for PNG or SVG. If the client only has a JPEG, rebuild it. A logo is the client's brand — delivering a white-box JPEG version is not acceptable work.
Essential Photopea Skills

items: Selection tools: Quick Selection, Magic Wand (select by colour), Lasso (freehand) — used to isolate parts of an image before editing
Remove background: Select subject → Select → Inverse → Delete. Or use Edit → Free Transform after isolating the subject on its own layer.
Crop and resize: Image → Image Size (for resolution changes), Image → Canvas Size (for cropping to a specific ratio), Crop tool (C) for manual cropping
Adjust brightness/contrast: Image → Adjustments → Brightness/Contrast (destructive) or use a Levels adjustment layer (non-destructive)
Export: File → Export As → choose format, set quality slider for JPEG/WebP lossy, confirm dimensions before saving

blocks.title: Prepare Three Product Images
blocks.intro: Complete this image preparation workflow for a web product launch.
steps: Open photopea.com in your browser. File → Open and load a product image (or use any photograph of an object).
Duplicate the background layer (right-click layer → Duplicate). This preserves the original.
Use the Quick Selection tool to select the product/subject area by clicking and dragging over it.
Go to Select → Inverse to select the background instead of the product.
Press Delete to remove the background. You should see a grey checkerboard pattern — this represents transparency.
Go to Image → Image Size. Set Width to 800px and Height to 800px. Make sure 'Constrain Proportions' is unchecked only if you need a square — otherwise let it scale proportionally then add canvas.
File → Export As → PNG. Save as product-1-web.png (for web with transparency).
Now File → Export As → WebP. Set Quality to 85. Save as product-1-web.webp.
Repeat for a second image. Compare file sizes between PNG and WebP versions.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SPOT THE QUALITY ISSUE
blocks.text:
You have been sent two versions of the same product photo:
- Version A: Original JPEG, 2 MB, sharp with no visible artefacts
- Version B: The same JPEG opened and re-saved 10 times at 60% quality, 180 KB

Without seeing the images, predict: Where would you expect to see quality degradation in Version B? Describe specifically what JPEG compression artefacts look like, where they appear first in a typical product photo, and what minimum quality setting you would use for a client's product images.

question.text: A client provides their logo as a JPEG with a white background. You need to place it on a dark blue website banner. The best solution is to:
options: Use the JPEG as-is — it will look fine on dark backgrounds
Export a WebP version of the JPEG for better compression
Obtain a PNG or SVG version with a transparent background
Add a dark blue border to the JPEG to blend it in
question.explanation: JPEG does not support transparency — placing it on a dark background shows the white rectangle around the logo. A PNG with an alpha channel (transparent background) or an SVG file will allow the logo to sit cleanly on any background colour.

question.text: A product image for a website needs to be high visual quality but load quickly. Which format and approach is most appropriate?
options: PNG at maximum quality — always use lossless
JPEG at 30% quality — smallest file size
WebP at 80–85% quality — balances quality and file size for web delivery
GIF — universally supported and small
question.explanation: WebP at 80–85% quality delivers file sizes 25–35% smaller than equivalent JPEG while maintaining visually indistinguishable quality. GIF has a 256-colour limit unsuitable for photos. PNG is lossless but produces large files for photos. JPEG at 30% shows significant artefacts.

question.text: A graphic designer creates a brochure for print at 72 PPI and the result looks blurry when the printer outputs it at A4 size. (a) Explain why this happened using the term PPI. (b) What resolution should have been used for print, and how would this affect the file size?
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: 72 PPI is the screen standard — when printed, the limited pixel count spreads across a larger physical area, resulting in visible pixelation and blur. PPI determines how many pixels are mapped per inch of physical output. Award 2 marks: Print requires a minimum of 300 PPI for sharp results. A 300 PPI image at A4 size (approximately 2480×3508 pixels) will have a significantly larger file size than a 72 PPI version of the same dimensions — approximately 17 times more pixel data.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4205 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 10 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

Our client tracker is a disaster. Client contact details live in three different email threads, Sloane's data sheet, and someone's notebook. We have lost two follow-up opportunities this month. Your task: build a shared client tracker in Google Sheets that any team member can update, search, and filter in under 10 seconds. This is a data problem and you are solving it.

A spreadsheet is not a database — but for small teams with hundreds of records, it often works well enough. Google Sheets gives IT teams a live, shareable, queryable data store that requires no server setup and integrates with Google Forms for data collection. Knowing when to use Sheets, when to upgrade to Airtable, and when to escalate to a proper SQL database is a skill that will save you and your client time and money.

items.definition: A single row in a database or spreadsheet — containing all the data about one entity (one client, one product, one order).
A single column in a database — representing one type of data for every record (client name, phone number, last contact date).
A unique identifier for each record that never changes and is never blank — ensures no two records are confused. Examples: client ID number, order number.
A Google Sheets / Excel formula that searches the first column of a table for a value and returns data from another column in the same row — used to pull related data from separate sheets.
A rule applied to a cell or column that restricts what values can be entered — e.g., a dropdown list, a date range, or a number-only field. Prevents incorrect data entry at the source.

blocks.text: Spreadsheet vs Database vs Airtable

rows: Small teams (<5), up to a few thousand records, shared live access
Slow with 10,000+ rows; no relational tables; limited access control
When records relate to each other (orders and clients) or multiple users need different permissions
Medium-sized operations needing relational views without SQL
Free tier limited; expensive at scale; not a true relational database
When you need SQL power with a spreadsheet interface and budget allows
Applications, large data volumes, complex relational queries
Requires technical setup and ongoing management
Any production application with real users

blocks.title: Build a Client Tracker in Google Sheets
blocks.intro: Build a functional client contact database with data validation, filtering, and a VLOOKUP formula.
steps: Open a new Google Sheet. Name the file 'Launchpad Client Tracker'.
In Row 1, create these column headers: Client ID | Company Name | Contact Name | Email | Phone | Industry | Last Contact Date | Status | Notes
Freeze Row 1: View → Freeze → 1 row. This keeps headers visible when scrolling.
Add data validation to the 'Status' column: select the whole column (click column letter), Data → Data Validation → Dropdown list: Active, Inactive, Lead, Proposal Sent
Add data validation to 'Last Contact Date': Data → Data Validation → Date is a valid date.
Enter 5 sample client records. Give each a unique Client ID (C001, C002, etc.).
Create a second sheet called 'Industries'. In column A, list: Technology, Healthcare, Education, Retail, Hospitality.
Return to the Client Tracker sheet. Add a VLOOKUP formula: in a new column 'Industry Code', enter =VLOOKUP(G2,Industries!A:A,1,FALSE) — this confirms the industry exists in your lookup table.
Enable filters: click anywhere in your data, Data → Create a Filter. Test by filtering to show only 'Active' clients.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The most expensive database is the one that lives in someone's inbox. Every business that says 'we manage it in email' has lost data, missed follow-ups, and frustrated clients they could not afford to lose. A shared Sheets tracker built in an afternoon is worth more than six months of email chaos.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SCHEMA FIRST
blocks.text:
Before building any database, a professional designs the schema — the structure of the data. You have been asked to build a tracker for a local surf school that needs to manage: student enrolments, instructor assignments, and lesson schedules.

On paper or in a doc, design the schema: (1) List the 3 tables/sheets you would create. (2) For each table, list the fields (columns) it would contain. (3) Identify the primary key for each table. (4) Identify which fields would link the tables together (foreign keys). Present as a simple diagram or table.

question.text: A VLOOKUP formula in Google Sheets is used to:
options: Create a dropdown validation list
Search a table for a matching value and return data from a specified column
Sort data alphabetically by column
Filter rows based on a condition
question.explanation: VLOOKUP (Vertical Lookup) searches the first column of a range for a matching value and returns data from a specified column in the same row. It is used to connect related data across different sheets or tables.

question.text: A business has 50,000 customer records with complex relationships between orders, products, and customer accounts. The most appropriate data storage solution would be:
options: Google Sheets with multiple tabs
A CSV file updated manually
An Airtable free tier account
A relational SQL database
question.explanation: At 50,000 records with complex relational needs, a relational SQL database (MySQL, PostgreSQL) is appropriate. Google Sheets performs poorly above ~10,000 rows; Airtable's free tier has record limits and pricing becomes prohibitive at scale; a CSV has no query capabilities.

question.text: A small business enters customer data into Google Sheets without any validation. After 6 months, the sheet contains: phone numbers in 5 different formats, 'N/A' in date fields, and company names spelled differently (e.g., 'City Café', 'City Cafe', 'city cafe'). (a) Identify the data quality problem this represents. (b) Describe two specific data validation rules that would have prevented these issues from the start.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: This represents dirty/inconsistent data — a data integrity problem caused by the absence of input constraints. Without validation, each person enters data in their own format, making the data unsearchable and unreliable for analysis or communication. Award 1 mark each for two valid validation solutions: (1) Phone number format — use Data Validation with a regex pattern or a custom formula to enforce a consistent format (e.g., 04XX XXX XXX); (2) Date fields — validate as 'date is a valid date' to prevent text entries like 'N/A'; (3) Company name — use a dropdown list of approved clients to prevent spelling variations.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4206 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 11 | Assigned to: Junior Analyst

The client's pitch is in 10 minutes. They called. PowerPoint is crashing every time they try to open the presentation file. The projector is connected. The client is panicking. You are the IT person. Fix it.

Software troubleshooting is not magic — it is a systematic process. The person who panics and randomly clicks things makes the problem worse. The person who breathes, identifies the symptoms, isolates the likely cause, and tests one fix at a time looks like a professional. The difference between those two people is knowing the process before you need it.
The Troubleshooting Process

items: IDENTIFY: What exactly is happening? Get the error message word for word. What were they doing when it happened? Is it reproducible?
ISOLATE: Is it this file or all files? This machine or all machines? This user or all users? Each answer narrows the cause.
RESEARCH: Google the exact error message. Check if there is a known issue, update, or forum thread. Do not spend more than 3 minutes on this before testing.
TEST ONE FIX: Apply one change and test. If you apply three fixes at once and it works, you will not know which one solved it.
DOCUMENT: Write down what the problem was, what you tried, and what fixed it. This becomes your knowledge base for next time.

blocks.text: Common Software Problems and First Fixes

rows: Corrupted file, missing update, or conflicting extension
Open a different file; run as administrator; check for pending updates
Too many processes, low RAM, malware, or storage nearly full
Task Manager → sort by CPU/RAM → close non-essential processes; check storage
DNS issue, cache problem, or browser extension conflict
Clear browser cache; try incognito mode; disable extensions; try a different browser
File will not open / shows wrong icon
Missing associated application or corrupted file
Check the file extension; try opening with a different application; verify the file is not corrupt
Driver issue, USB connection, or Windows update conflict
Unplug and replug USB; check Device Manager for errors; reinstall driver
Saved to wrong location, or OneDrive sync issue
Search file name in Windows search; check Recent Files in the application; check OneDrive
No admin rights, or file locked by another process
Run as administrator; check if the file is open in another program; log out and back in

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: 'Have you tried turning it off and on again' is a joke that is also genuinely true. A restart clears RAM, terminates stuck processes, applies pending updates, and re-establishes network connections. It fixes roughly 30% of all software problems. Do it first — before you spend 45 minutes diagnosing a problem that a reboot would have solved in 90 seconds.
Browser Troubleshooting

items: Clear cache and cookies: Settings → Clear browsing data → select cache and cookies → Last 24 hours or All time
Try incognito/private mode: Opens without extensions or cached data — if the problem disappears in incognito, an extension is the likely cause
Disable extensions: Settings → Extensions → disable all, then re-enable one at a time to identify the problem extension
Try a different browser: If Chrome fails, try Edge or Firefox. If the problem follows you across browsers, it is not a browser issue — it is a network or server issue
Check the URL: Is it HTTP when HTTPS is required? Is there a typo?

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · WORK THE SCENARIOS
blocks.text:
For each scenario, write: (1) the troubleshooting step you take first, (2) what information you gather to isolate the cause, (3) the most likely fix:

Scenario A: A student's Google Slides presentation was working yesterday. Today, every image shows a broken link icon.

Scenario B: A teacher clicks 'Print' but the printer is not listed in the print dialog, even though it was working last week.

Scenario C: A Google Form the class has been using starts returning a 'This form is no longer accepting responses' error message mid-session.

Document each as: Problem → Likely Cause → First Fix → How to verify it worked.

question.text: A user reports their browser loads YouTube but not their company's internal website. The most useful next troubleshooting step is:
options: Reinstall the browser
Try the internal website in incognito mode
Try the internal website in a different browser
Check whether the internal website loads on a different device on the same network
question.explanation: Checking a different device on the same network isolates whether the problem is the website/server, the network, or the specific machine/browser. If it works on another device, the problem is local to the original machine. If it fails everywhere, the problem is the server or network.

question.text: When troubleshooting a software issue, applying multiple fixes simultaneously is the fastest way to resolve the problem.
question.explanation: False. Applying multiple fixes at once means you cannot identify which fix solved the problem. This creates a knowledge gap that will slow you down the next time the same issue occurs. Test one change at a time, then document what worked.

question.text: A colleague says their Excel file 'just disappeared' — they cannot find it anywhere on their computer. Describe a structured three-step troubleshooting process to help them locate or recover the file, identifying what you would check at each step and why.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark each for three logical steps: (1) Search using Windows Search — type the filename; check Recent Files in Excel (File → Recent) which lists the last 25 files regardless of where they were saved. (2) Check the default save location — Documents, Desktop, OneDrive — and sort by date modified to find recently changed files. (3) Check OneDrive Version History or Windows Previous Versions (right-click folder → Properties → Previous Versions) in case the file was overwritten or deleted from a synced folder. Also check the Recycle Bin.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4207 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 12 | Project Milestone

Congratulations — you have just been given your first project ownership. A local community group wants a simple interactive quiz embedded on their website: users answer 5 questions and get a personalised result. You have 4 weeks and a small team. You have worked across enough deliverables this sprint to know the tools. Now you need to plan before you build. Nobody ships without a brief and a timeline.

An interactive project is any digital product where user input changes what happens next — a quiz with personalised results, a survey that branches based on answers, a calculator, a booking form. Planning these projects follows the same professional process regardless of the size: brief, scope, wireframe, task list, timeline. Skipping any step always costs more time than it saves.

items.definition: A short document that defines the problem, the target audience, the required features, the constraints (budget, deadline, technology), and the success criteria. Every project starts here.
The defined boundary of what the project will and will not include. Scope creep — features added without adjusting the timeline — is the most common reason projects run late.
A low-fidelity visual layout showing the structure and placement of elements on each screen, without colour, fonts, or final content. Used to agree on structure before committing to design.
The smallest version of a product that still fulfils its core purpose and can be tested with users. Building MVP first prevents wasted effort on features no one needs.
A bar chart showing project tasks on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis. Each bar shows when a task starts and ends, making overlaps and dependencies visible.

blocks.text: Step 1: Write the Project Brief

A project brief answers six questions before any code or design begins. For the community quiz project, the brief might read: 'Problem: The group lacks a way to engage new visitors on their website. Audience: Adults 18–50 in Bunbury, not necessarily tech-savvy. Features: 5-question multiple-choice quiz, personalised result message (3 possible outcomes). Constraints: Must work on mobile, use free tools only, ready in 4 weeks. Success criteria: At least 60% of quiz takers reach the results screen. Out of scope: User accounts, leaderboards, email collection.'

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The brief is the only document where the client cannot change their mind for free. Every feature added after the brief is signed costs time and usually money. I have never had a client who did not want to add something halfway through — that is human nature. But if the brief is solid and both parties signed off, that conversation becomes 'we can do that in the next sprint' instead of 'we are now 2 weeks late.'
Step 2: Wireframe Before You Build

items: Wireframes are quick: A 3-screen wireframe can be sketched in 15 minutes. That 15 minutes saves hours of rebuilding screens after the client sees something they did not expect.
Lo-fi is fine at this stage: Boxes, lines, and labels. No colour, no final fonts. The goal is agreement on structure, not approval of aesthetics.
Tools: Paper and pencil, Google Slides, draw.io (free), or Figma (free for students). The tool does not matter — the thinking does.
Label interactions: Show what happens when the user clicks a button. An arrow from 'Submit' to 'Results Screen' makes the flow clear without writing a line of code.
Get sign-off before designing: Show the wireframe to the client (or teacher) and confirm the structure before investing design time.

blocks.title: Write a Brief and Wireframe for Your Interactive Project
blocks.intro: Complete this planning sequence for a simple 5-question quiz for a community group.
steps: Open Google Docs. Create a document titled 'Project Brief — Community Quiz'.
Write the six sections: Problem, Target Audience, Features (list 3–5), Constraints, Success Criteria, Out of Scope.
For Features, distinguish between MVP (what must be there) and Nice to Have (what could be added in a future sprint).

On a new page or in draw.io, sketch three screens: (1) Welcome screen with a Start button, (2) Question screen with question text, 4 answer options, and a Next button, (3) Results screen with a personalised message based on the score.
On the wireframe, add arrows showing navigation flow: Welcome → Question 1 → ... → Question 5 → Results.
Share the brief and wireframe with a classmate. Ask: 'Would you know what to build from this?' Revise based on their response.

blocks.text: Step 3: Build a Timeline

rows: Brief finalised, wireframes approved, tool selected (e.g. Google Forms + logic, or Genially)
Build quiz structure, write all 5 questions and 3 result messages
Apply design (colours, fonts, images), test on mobile, fix bugs
User testing with 3 people outside the team, final fixes, embed on test page

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · BUILD YOUR GANTT
blocks.text:
Using Google Sheets or draw.io, create a Gantt chart for your 4-week quiz project. It must show:
- All tasks from the timeline above, broken into daily or every-2-day blocks
- Which tasks can run in parallel (e.g., design and writing questions) and which must wait (e.g., testing can only happen after building)
- Who is responsible for each task (use your group member names)
- One buffer day per week — because things always take longer than expected

Colour code: Planning (blue), Building (orange), Design (green), Testing (red)

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
1. Halfway through Week 2, the client emails asking if you can add a leaderboard that saves high scores. You said yes. Explain exactly what went wrong in this conversation and what you should have said instead.
2. Your wireframe shows a 'Previous' button on the question screen, but you forgot to show what happens if the user clicks it on Question 1. What document should capture this edge case, and why is it a risk to leave it unresolved before building?
3. Your MVP is a working 5-question quiz. Your teammate suggests 'while we're at it' adding animations, a countdown timer, and social media sharing before the deadline. Apply the MoSCoW method to these three features and explain your reasoning.

question.text: In project management, 'scope creep' refers to:
options: The gradual increase in project team size over time
Features or requirements being added after the project brief is finalised without adjusting the timeline
The project delivery date being moved earlier than planned
The client reducing the features requested mid-project
question.explanation: Scope creep is the gradual addition of features, changes, or requirements beyond the original agreed scope — without corresponding adjustments to timeline, budget, or resources. It is one of the most common causes of project delays and overruns.

question.text: What is the primary purpose of a low-fidelity wireframe in the planning phase?
options: To show the client the final colour scheme and font choices
To agree on screen structure and user flow before investing in design
To replace the project brief with a visual document
To test whether the code runs correctly
question.explanation: Low-fidelity wireframes show structure — what appears on each screen and how screens connect — not aesthetics. They allow all stakeholders to agree on layout and flow cheaply, before design or development time is invested.

question.text: A team is building a booking system for a local gym. They jump straight into coding without a project brief or wireframes. Two weeks later, the client says 'I thought there would be a way for members to cancel their own bookings' — a feature not discussed. (a) What planning step would have captured this requirement before development started? (b) What is the professional way to respond to the client's request given it was not in the original scope?
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: A project brief with a complete features list AND wireframes showing the user's full journey through the booking process would have prompted the client to review and approve each screen — making the absence of a cancellation feature visible and resolvable before coding. Award 2 marks: The professional response acknowledges the request without agreeing to absorb the work for free: 'That's a great feature and we can absolutely add it. As it's outside the original scope, we'll need to discuss extending the timeline by [X days] and adjusting the project cost. I'll send you a revised brief for your approval.'

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4208 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟢 SPRINT REVIEW | Sprint 12 | Assessment

This is your end-of-sprint assessment. Rory reviews every intern on how well they can apply what they have learned — not just recall it. These questions reflect the kind of decisions you will make on the job. No notes, no Googling. Show your thinking.

blocks.heading: 📋 Unit 2 Application Skills — What You've Covered
blocks.text:
This topic covered seven professional tool sets:
1. Presentation Software Features — Slide Masters, transitions, Presenter View
2. Creating and Presenting — 6×6 rule, embedded charts, delivery skills
3. Audio Software — Audacity workflow, file formats, WAV vs MP3
4. Image Manipulation — Photopea, raster vs vector, format selection, background removal
5. Online Database Tools — Google Sheets, VLOOKUP, data validation, when to use SQL
6. Software Troubleshooting — systematic process, common fixes, documentation
7. Planning an Interactive Project — brief, wireframe, MVP, Gantt chart

question.text: Editing the Slide Master in a 30-slide PowerPoint affects:
options: Only the currently selected slide
All slides that use the default layout
Only title slides
No slides — the Slide Master is for reference only
question.explanation: The Slide Master is a global template — changes to it (fonts, colours, logos) propagate automatically to all slides using that layout, eliminating the need for manual updates.

question.text: A WAV file and an MP3 file of the same recording will have identical file sizes.
question.explanation: False. WAV is an uncompressed, lossless format and is significantly larger than MP3. A 3-minute WAV at 44.1 kHz / 16-bit is approximately 30 MB; the same recording as MP3 at 192 kbps is approximately 4.3 MB.

question.text: A client provides a logo as a JPEG for use on a dark website background. The main problem with using a JPEG logo is:
options: JPEG files are too large for web use
JPEG does not support transparency
JPEG cannot be displayed in a browser
JPEG logos always appear blurry
question.explanation: JPEG does not support an alpha channel (transparency). A JPEG logo on a dark background displays with a white rectangle around it. PNG or SVG formats with transparent backgrounds are required.

question.text: In software troubleshooting, applying multiple fixes at the same time is the most efficient approach when the issue is urgent.
question.explanation: False. Applying multiple fixes simultaneously makes it impossible to identify what actually solved the problem, preventing you from building a reliable knowledge base. Even under time pressure, test changes one at a time.

question.text: VLOOKUP in Google Sheets is primarily used to:
options: Sort data alphabetically
Pull related data from another table using a matching value
Validate that data entries are in the correct format
Create pivot table summaries
question.explanation: VLOOKUP searches the first column of a range for a specified value and returns data from a specified column in the same row — used to connect related data across sheets without manually copying it.

question.text: The purpose of a project brief is to agree on the scope of work before development begins, protecting both the client and the team from later disagreements about what was promised.
question.explanation: True. A project brief defines the problem, features, constraints, and success criteria. It protects the client from receiving something different from what they expected, and protects the team from scope creep and unpaid additional work.

question.text: The 6×6 rule in presentation design states that slides should have:
options: 6 slides per presentation and 6 minutes of speaking time
6 bullet points maximum per slide and 6 words maximum per bullet
6 images per slide and 6 colours in the palette
6 fonts maximum and 6 animation effects per slide
question.explanation: The 6×6 rule: no more than 6 bullet points per slide, no more than 6 words per bullet. This prevents slides from becoming text walls and forces the presenter to communicate detail verbally rather than reading from the screen.

question.text: A low-fidelity wireframe should show the final colours, fonts, and imagery of the finished product.
question.explanation: False. A low-fidelity wireframe shows structure and layout only — boxes, labels, and arrows — without final design elements. The purpose is to agree on structure cheaply before committing time to visual design.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4301 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 1 — Team Onboarding

Welcome to your first team project. You are not working solo anymore — this is a 4-person sprint. Before a single line gets written or a pixel gets pushed, every person on this team needs to understand how the Launchpad takes a client brief from a napkin sketch to a shipped product. This is that briefing.

The design process is a structured, iterative method for solving problems through digital products. Every professional IT team follows some version of it — whether they call it Agile, Design Thinking, or the SDLC. The specific names vary; the underlying logic does not. You discover the problem, define the solution, design it, build it, deliver it, and evaluate it. Then you do it again, better.

items.definition: A structured, repeatable sequence of phases used to move from a client's problem to a tested digital solution: Discover → Define → Design → Develop → Deliver → Evaluate.
Repeating a phase of the design process based on feedback or testing results. Professional products go through multiple iterations — version 1.0 is never the final answer.
An early, simplified version of a product built to test an idea before full development. Can be a paper sketch, a clickable wireframe, or partial working code.
A concrete output produced at the end of each phase — a brief, a wireframe, a tested prototype, a deployed product. Deliverables prove that each phase was completed.
The structured process of gathering user or client reactions to a prototype or product and using those reactions to inform the next iteration.

blocks.text: The Six Phases and Their Deliverables

rows: Research the problem — interview users, review existing solutions, identify pain points
Research summary, user insights, problem statement
Agree on exactly what you are solving and for whom. Write the project brief.
Signed project brief, scope document, success criteria
Wireframe screens, choose tools, agree on visual direction
Approved wireframes, style guide, feature list
Build the product — code, configure, populate with content
Test with real users, fix bugs, hand over to client
Tested product, user testing report, handover notes
Review: did it meet the brief? What would you do differently?
Evaluation report, lessons learned, next-sprint recommendation

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The phase most teams skip is Discover. They think they already know the problem. They are wrong. I have watched teams build technically excellent solutions to the wrong problem. Spending two hours talking to actual users before you write a line of code is the most valuable two hours in the project. Do not negotiate it away.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · MAP YOUR PROCESS
blocks.text:
You have been asked to build a simple interactive quiz for a local community group (sound familiar?). Map out what you would do in each of the six phases for this specific project:

For each phase: (1) List one specific action you would take. (2) Name the deliverable you would produce. (3) Estimate how long it would take in hours or days.

Present as a table. When you are done, identify which phase will be most challenging for your team and why.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
1. A team skips the Define phase and goes straight from Discover to Design. Three weeks later, the client says the product does not match what they asked for. What specifically went wrong, and which deliverable from the Define phase would have prevented this?
2. After delivering the product, the client reports that one core feature is difficult to use. Under the design process, which phase deals with this, and what should the team produce in response?
3. What is the difference between a prototype and the final delivered product? Give a specific example of each for the community quiz project.

question.text: Which phase of the design process involves gathering information about the problem from users and clients before proposing any solutions?
options: Define
Develop
Discover
Deliver
question.explanation: The Discover phase focuses on research — understanding the problem from the user's perspective, identifying pain points, and gathering evidence before any solutions are proposed. Jumping to solutions without this phase is one of the most common causes of building the wrong product.

question.text: A team releases a product, collects user feedback, makes improvements, and releases a second version. This process is best described as:
options: Scope creep
Iteration
The Define phase
A Gantt chart
question.explanation: Iteration is the process of repeating a cycle — building, testing, learning, improving — based on feedback. Professional software products are never 'finished' in a single pass; they improve through repeated iterations.

question.text: A client tells a student team: 'Just build me something — I'll know what I want when I see it.' (a) What risk does this approach create for the team? (b) Which phase of the design process exists specifically to prevent this situation, and what document does that phase produce? (c) Describe what that document should include.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: Without clear requirements upfront, the team has no definition of success — the client can always say 'this isn't what I meant' regardless of what is built. Award 1 mark: The Define phase. Award 2 marks: The project brief/scope document should include the problem being solved, target audience, specific features (in scope), what will NOT be included (out of scope), constraints (timeline, tools, budget), and measurable success criteria.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4302 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 1 — Day 3

The feature list for the community quiz project is now confirmed. You have 4 weeks and 4 team members. The client brief has 11 tasks. Nobody on the team has done this before. Your job today: plan the sprint before the chaos starts. I need a Trello board with every task assigned and due by end of today.

Time is the only resource in a project that cannot be recovered when it is wasted. Money can be reallocated; scope can be reduced; deadlines rarely move. Professionals who manage their time well do not work harder than those who do not — they work with a clearer picture of what matters most, what can wait, and what has to be cut.

items.definition: A prioritisation tool that sorts tasks by urgency (time-sensitive) and importance (high impact). Four quadrants: Do Now (urgent+important), Schedule (important, not urgent), Delegate (urgent, not important), Eliminate (neither).
A project prioritisation framework: Must Have (essential for launch), Should Have (important but not critical), Could Have (nice-to-have if time permits), Won't Have (excluded from this version).
A fixed time period (typically 1–2 weeks) in which a team commits to completing a defined set of tasks. At the end of the sprint, work is reviewed and the next sprint is planned.
A visual project timeline: tasks on the vertical axis, time on the horizontal axis. Bars show each task's start and end date. Dependencies between tasks are shown with arrows.

blocks.text: The Eisenhower Matrix in Practice

rows: Client meeting today, site is down, deadline is tomorrow
Block time for it this week
Writing quiz questions, designing the results screen
Replying to a client CC email, attending a non-essential meeting
Adding social media sharing before the MVP is even working

blocks.text: MoSCoW for the Quiz Project

items: Must Have: 5 working questions, 3 personalised result messages, mobile-responsive layout, works without logging in
Should Have: Progress indicator (Question 2 of 5), restart quiz button, readable on both light and dark screens
Could Have: Animated transitions between questions, share result on social media
Won't Have (this sprint): User accounts, leaderboard, result email capture

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Your first estimate will be wrong. Your second estimate will be less wrong. After ten projects, you will still underestimate by 20%. Build buffer into your schedule — not as a sign of poor planning, but as a sign of experience. A schedule with no slack is not a plan; it is a wish.

blocks.title: Set Up a Project Trello Board
blocks.intro: Create a digital task board for the community quiz project using Trello's Kanban structure.
steps: Go to trello.com and create a free account if you do not have one.
Create a new board called 'Community Quiz — Bunbury Tech Launchpad'.
Create four lists: Backlog | In Progress | Review | Done
Add one card (task) per feature from the project's Must Have list. Give each card a descriptive title.
Open each card and add: a due date, the name of the person responsible, and a checklist of 2–3 sub-steps.
Drag your top-priority task to 'In Progress'.
Add two cards from the Should Have list to the Backlog.
Take a screenshot of the board to attach to your project brief as evidence of sprint planning.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · EISENHOWER YOUR LIST
blocks.text:
Apply the Eisenhower Matrix to the following 8 tasks for the quiz project. Assign each to one of the four quadrants and give one sentence of reasoning:

1. Fix the bug where Question 3 always shows the wrong answer
2. Design a beautiful animated loading screen
3. Reply to the client's email about the launch date (she needs confirmation today)
4. Write the five quiz questions and answer options
5. Set up the shared Trello board before the team meeting
6. Research whether social media sharing buttons would increase engagement
7. Build the quiz's results screen logic
8. Check that the quiz works on an iPhone (client specifically mentioned mobile)

question.text: In the MoSCoW method, a 'Could Have' feature is best described as:
options: A feature that must be present for the product to function
A feature that is desirable but will be excluded if time runs short
A feature that will definitely be delivered in the next version
A feature that has been permanently removed from the project
question.explanation: A 'Could Have' feature is a nice-to-have that the team would like to include but will cut if necessary to meet the deadline with the 'Must Have' and 'Should Have' features intact. It is not a promise — it is conditional on available time.

question.text: A task that is urgent but not important should be completed by the team leader personally, as urgency indicates high priority.
question.explanation: False. In the Eisenhower Matrix, tasks that are urgent but not important should be delegated to another team member — not necessarily the leader. The leader's time should be protected for important tasks. Urgency alone is not a measure of importance.

question.text: A 4-week project team has 3 Must Have features and 5 Should Have features. At the end of Week 3, the Must Haves are complete but 4 of the 5 Should Haves remain unfinished. (a) Using the MoSCoW method, what decision should the team make? (b) How should the team communicate this to the client, and what evidence from their planning process helps support the conversation?
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: The MoSCoW method exists precisely for this scenario — Should Haves are deliberately deprioritised relative to Must Haves. The team should deliver the 3 Must Haves on deadline and formally note the 4 remaining Should Haves as candidates for the next sprint, not failures. Award 2 marks: The team should communicate proactively (not wait for the client to ask), reference the original brief where Should Haves were explicitly categorised as 'important but not critical,' and propose a next sprint to address the remaining items. The brief and sprint plan are the evidence.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4303 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 2 — Design Phase

The client has approved the project brief. Now they want to see what it will look like before we build it. Your job: produce a design representation clear enough that the client — who is not technical — can understand exactly what they are getting, screen by screen. No guessing. No 'trust me, it'll look good.' Show them.

Design representations are the visual language of IT projects. They let a team show, rather than describe, what a product will look like and how it will behave — before any code is written. The right representation at the right fidelity level saves enormous amounts of rework. The wrong representation (too detailed too early, or too vague too late) creates confusion and missed expectations.

items.definition: A low-fidelity layout showing the structure and content placement on each screen without colour, fonts, or imagery. The skeleton of a digital product.
A high-fidelity static visual showing the final appearance of each screen — colours, fonts, imagery, spacing — without interactive behaviour.
A clickable or interactive model that simulates how the product will behave, allowing users to test flows without working code.
The level of visual and functional detail in a design representation. Low-fidelity = rough sketches; high-fidelity = near-final appearance and interaction.
A diagram using standard symbols (rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions, arrows for flow) to map out the steps and logic of a system or user journey.

blocks.text: Types of Design Representation

rows: First 10 minutes of the Design phase — rapid idea generation
Getting client agreement on screen structure before visual design
Client approval of visual design before development begins
User testing — simulating the real product experience without code
Mapping user journeys, decision logic, or system data flow
Planning video, animation, or multi-screen interaction sequences

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: Draw it before you build it, or you will build it twice. I have never seen a team spend too long on wireframes. I have watched teams spend six weeks rebuilding a product because the client saw the first version and said 'that is not what I imagined.' A wireframe costs 30 minutes. A rebuild costs 3 weeks.

blocks.title: Create a Three-Screen Wireframe
blocks.intro: Produce a wireframe for a simple event RSVP application using boxes, labels, and arrows.
steps: Open diagrams.net in your browser (free, no login required). Choose 'Blank Diagram'.
Draw Screen 1 — Welcome: A title ('Community Event RSVP'), one sentence of intro text, and a large 'Register Now' button. Use rectangles for text blocks and a filled rectangle for the button.
Draw Screen 2 — Registration Form: Fields for First Name, Last Name, Email, Number of guests (dropdown). A 'Submit' button at the bottom.
Draw Screen 3 — Confirmation: 'Thank you!' heading, a summary of the registration details, and a 'Add to Calendar' button.
Connect the screens with arrows: 'Register Now' → Screen 2; 'Submit' → Screen 3.
Add a label on the Submit arrow: '→ Validates form; shows error if fields are empty'.
Label each screen with its name at the top (e.g., '1. Welcome Screen').
Export as PNG and paste into your project documentation.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · FLOWCHART THE LOGIC
blocks.text:
The community quiz has this logic: Users answer 5 questions. If they score 0–2 correct: show Result A. If they score 3–4: show Result B. If they score 5: show Result C. All users see a 'Try Again' button.

Using draw.io or paper, create a flowchart that maps this complete logic. Use:
- Rectangles for processes ('Show Question 1', 'Add 1 to score', 'Show Result A')
- Diamonds for decisions ('Is answer correct?', 'Is this the last question?', 'What is the final score?')
- Arrows with Yes/No labels on decisions

Your flowchart must be complete enough that a developer could build the quiz from it without asking you any clarifying questions.

question.text: A team is in the first day of the Design phase and needs to get client agreement on screen structure quickly. The most appropriate design representation is:
options: A high-fidelity Figma mockup with final colours and fonts
A clickable prototype built in Marvel
A low-fidelity digital wireframe using boxes and labels
The final coded product deployed to a test server
question.explanation: A low-fidelity wireframe is the most appropriate at this stage — it communicates structure quickly and cheaply without investing design time that may be wasted if the client requests changes to the layout.

question.text: Which design representation would be most appropriate for user testing — allowing users to experience the app's navigation without working code?
options: A paper sketch
A system flowchart
A clickable prototype
A data-flow diagram
question.explanation: A clickable prototype (built in Figma, Marvel, or similar tools) allows users to tap/click through screens and experience the navigation flow without any real code behind it. This is the standard tool for usability testing before development.

question.text: A development team shows the client a finished, fully coded product for the first time. The client says the layout is completely wrong — buttons are in the wrong places and the signup form should come before the feature overview, not after. (a) At which point in the design process should this feedback have been gathered? (b) Which design representation, if used correctly, would have prevented this outcome? Explain how.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks: This feedback should have been gathered during the Design phase, after producing wireframes and before any development began. Wireframes exist precisely to validate screen layout and navigation structure at a stage where changes are cheap. Award 2 marks: A low-fidelity wireframe showing all screens and the navigation flow between them would have made the button placement and screen order visible to the client. Client sign-off on the wireframe is agreement on structure — any layout complaint after that point is a scope change, not a free fix.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #4304 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 2 — Design Review

Leo has handed you the visual design draft for the community quiz. Before it goes to the client, it goes through a design review. Your task: evaluate the design against the principles of balance, contrast, emphasis, unity, and proportion — and against accessibility standards. If it fails any of these checks, the design does not leave this room.

Design elements and principles are not just for graphic designers — they are the shared vocabulary IT teams use when reviewing digital products. When Rory says 'the call-to-action button is not getting enough emphasis' or 'the colour contrast is going to fail WCAG,' everyone on the team needs to know what that means and what to do about it.

items.definition: The arrangement of elements so that the most important content is seen first — achieved through size, colour, contrast, and position.
Empty space around and between elements. Whitespace is not wasted — it gives elements room to breathe, improves readability, and draws attention to what remains.
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — the international standard for accessible digital content. AA level requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.
A numerical measure of the difference in luminance between two colours — foreground text and its background. A higher ratio means more readable text for users with vision impairments.

blocks.text: Applying the Principles to Digital Products

rows: Visual weight distributed evenly across the screen
Centre-align key content; use grid layouts so elements don't cluster on one side
Every element left-aligned, creating heavy left side and empty right
One element visually stands out as the most important
The primary call-to-action button is larger, brighter, or higher contrast than surrounding elements
Making everything bold — nothing stands out when everything competes
Differences between elements to create visual interest and ensure readability
Dark text on light background (or vice versa); WCAG AA contrast ratio minimum 4.5:1
Grey text on white background — fails contrast check and is unreadable in sunlight
All elements feel like they belong to the same system
Consistent font, colour palette (2–3 colours), spacing, and button styles throughout
Different button styles on every screen — looks like multiple products stitched together
